target.title: fix(cron): capture originating session/agent on the cron wake tool call
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** The cron `wake` MCP tool forwards only `{mode, text}` to the gateway, so every wake enqueues against the heartbeat/main default. Wakes scheduled from a non-main session (Telegram topic, Discord thread, multi-agent setup) silently route to the wrong conversation lane — the event text never returns to the conversation that scheduled it. Closes the tool-path half of #46886 / #64556.
- **What changed.**
  - `cron-tool.ts` wake action resolves the calling agent's `sessionKey`/`agentId` via the existing `resolveInternalSessionKey` / `resolveSessionAgentId` pattern (mirrors the `add` action). Explicit tool params take precedence; an explicit cross-agent `sessionKey` derives its `agentId` from the key itself (`parseAgentSessionKey`) so the caller's agentId is never paired with a foreign session.
  - `WakeParamsSchema` (+ regenerated Swift protocol client), gateway wake handler, cron service contract, and `wake()` thread the optional `sessionKey`/`agentId` through to `enqueueSystemEvent` and the heartbeat request.
  - `wake()` carries the originating session's **stored channel delivery context** (e.g. the bound Telegram `message_thread_id`) onto the system event via an optional `resolveOriginDeliveryContext` dep, implemented in `server-cron.ts` from the **session store** (not by string-splitting the session key) — a wake-now heartbeat replies in the originating topic, not the chat root. No-origin wakes keep the exact pre-fix `enqueueSystemEvent(text, undefined)` shape.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Rebase onto current main (this push)

- Rebased onto current `main`. Main has since absorbed the *scheduled-job* half of this design natively: `sessionTarget:"main"` cron jobs now run through the dedicated `agent:<id>:cron:<job>:run:<ts>` lane and borrow the target session's delivery context (`resolveMainSessionCronDeliveryContext`). **This PR therefore no longer touches `executeMainSessionCronJob`/the run lane at all** — it layers the same origin-capture idea onto the manual `wake` path only, which main still lacks. (This resolves the earlier ClawSweeper [P1] "Preserve main cron run lanes" by construction.)
- `wake()` moved to `src/cron/service/wake.ts` upstream; the change follows it there.
- Swift gateway protocol client regenerated (`pnpm protocol:gen && pnpm protocol:gen:swift`) for the new `WakeParams.agentId` field — resolves the earlier [P2].

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A cron `wake` scheduled from a non-main Telegram forum-topic session must enqueue against the originating session, and the wake-triggered heartbeat turn must deliver its reply back into that same topic instead of the heartbeat/main default (tool-path half of #46886 / #64556).
- **Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw gateway built from this head (`pnpm build:docker`), real Telegram Bot API (dedicated test bot) and a real Telegram Desktop client driven by the tdlib user driver, running in a Linux container; `claude-cli` (claude-sonnet-4-6) agent backend; BEFORE (`57633c42b`, current main) and AFTER (this branch) builds captured with identical config including `agents.defaults.heartbeat: {"every":"1h","target":"last"}`. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop-proof flow (telegram-desktop + user-driver + ffmpeg screen recording).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** In a fresh forum topic, the driver account sends: *"I am testing my cron wake routing in this topic. Please schedule a wake for right now using your cron tool (action wake, mode now) with text: You were woken by a cron wake. Run date -u with your exec tool and reply with exactly PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at followed by the command output. After your wake tool call returns, reply only: scheduled."* — recorded by ffmpeg with the gateway at debug log level.
- **Evidence after fix:** screen recording [after-83738v2-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-small.gif) and tool-bearing wake-turn recording [after-83738v3-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-small.gif); screenshots [after v2 still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-full.png), [after v3 still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-full.png); raw mp4s [v2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-raw.mp4), [v3](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-raw.mp4); redacted runtime logs [gw-after v2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v2.log), [gw-after v3](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v3.log).
- **Observed result after fix:** the turn reply "scheduled." lands in the topic (`outbound send ok … threadId=74 messageId=76`); the wake fires an immediate heartbeat turn ON the topic session (`trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present`); that second agent turn runs `date -u` via the exec tool and its reply — "PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at Wed Jun 10 04:43:21 UTC 2026" — is delivered into the SAME topic (`threadId=74 messageId=77`). Live command output in the reply demonstrates a real freshly-executed agent turn, not a delayed canned message.
- **Before evidence (optional):** [before-83738v2-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-small.gif), [before still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-full.png), [before mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-raw.mp4), [gw-before log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-before-83738v2.log) — same scenario on current main: "scheduled." arrives, then the wake-triggered heartbeat runs detached (`historyPrompt=none`) and its reply is never delivered to the topic or the chat root.
- **What was not tested:** Discord/Matrix live wake routing (the deliveryContext path is channel-generic; pinned by `wake-origin-delivery.test.ts`); live cross-agent wakes (the agentId-derivation contract is pinned by the cron-tool wake-routing unit tests).

### Current-head proof — no heartbeat target configured (the `a53fddb9f59` fallback)

Driven on the exact PR head with **no `agents.defaults.heartbeat` block at all**, via the same `wake` RPC Mantis uses (`system event --mode now --session-key <topic>`). The wake-triggered turn runs `date -u` and its reply lands back in the originating forum topic — proving the new no-heartbeat-target fallback:

![no-target wake reply in topic](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-small.gif)

Reply captured live: **"PROOF-83738 no-target wake ran at Wed Jun 10 13:14:33 UTC 2026"** ([full still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-full.png) · [mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-raw.mp4)). Gateway debug log ([gw-notarget.log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-notarget.log)) shows the chain with no heartbeat target set:

```
[telegram] Inbound …topic:130 …                                     <- seed turn
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec … trigger=user …
[telegram] outbound send ok … threadId=130                          <- seed reply "ready"
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec … trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present   <- wake turn ran ON the topic session
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=133 … threadId=130          <- wake reply, SAME topic
```

**`target: "none"` still suppresses** ([gw-tnone.log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-tnone.log)): with `heartbeat.target: "none"`, the same wake fires the heartbeat turn (`trigger=heartbeat`, `outBytes=12`) but produces **zero** `outbound send` — operator opt-out preserved.

### Proof detail — Mantis-flow recreation

This is a recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop-proof flow using the patched local crabbox (same mechanism: real Telegram Desktop client + tdlib user driver + ffmpeg screen capture in a Linux container; both builds, same config incl. `agents.defaults.heartbeat: {"every":"1h","target":"last"}`, bot + group ids are test-only). Scenario: a user in a **forum topic** asks the agent to schedule `cron wake (mode "now")`; the wake text instructs the heartbeat to reply in-thread.

| | BEFORE (current main, `57633c42b`) | AFTER (this branch, `880d10a34`) |
|---|---|---|
| turn reply ("scheduled.") | ✅ in topic | ✅ in topic |
| wake-triggered heartbeat reply | ❌ **never delivered anywhere** (not topic, not chat root) | ✅ **"PROOF-83738 wake delivered." lands back in the same topic** |

| BEFORE | AFTER |
|---|---|
| ![before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-small.gif) | ![after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-small.gif) |

**The wake starts a real second agent TURN, not a delayed canned reply** — in this capture the wake text instructs the woken turn to run `date -u` via the exec tool, and the in-topic reply embeds the live command output ("PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at Wed Jun 10 04:43:21 UTC 2026"), which only a freshly-executed agent turn can produce:

![wake turn runs a tool](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-small.gif)

([full still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-full.png) · [mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-raw.mp4) · [gateway debug log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v3.log))

Full-window stills: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-full.png) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-full.png) · raw mp4s: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-raw.mp4) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-raw.mp4) · redacted gateway logs: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-before-83738v2.log) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v2.log)

Key gateway-log lines (AFTER build, debug level):

```
[telegram] Inbound message telegram:group:-100…:topic:74 -> @…2bot (group, 227 chars)
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec: … trigger=user …
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=76 … threadId=74            <- turn reply "scheduled."
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec: … trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present   <- the wake fired, ON the topic session
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=77 … threadId=74            <- wake reply, SAME topic
```

Same scenario on BEFORE: the heartbeat fires with `historyPrompt=none` on a detached lane and no outbound send ever follows — the wake is lost.

A deterministic harness driving the real gateway handler + cron service with logging deps is included (`pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts`): origin routing, backwards-compatible default routing, the next-heartbeat targeted-immediate collapse, the subagent guard, and whitespace fall-through.

## Mutation testing (focused Stryker on the PR-touched code)

- `src/cron/service/wake.ts`: **51/51 mutants killed (100%)**. Two initial survivors exposed real test gaps (agentId-only wakes; sessionKey-only delivery-context resolution) — both now pinned by dedicated tests.
- `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts` wake arm (L840–893): **26/31 killed (83.9%)**. All 5 survivors are test-harness equivalents, not behavior gaps: the suite `vi.mock`s `resolveSessionAgentId` to a constant (arg mutations invisible), the `mode` fallback equals the compared literal, and `{expectFinal:false}` is invisible to the gateway mock. The killable gaps found on the way (wake-without-text guard, context-less callers, explicit next-heartbeat mode) got tests.

## Tests / CI

- New/updated: `wake-origin.test.ts`, `wake-origin-delivery.test.ts`, cron-tool wake-routing suite (10 cases), `validateWakeParams` schema cases. All wake/cron/protocol suites green on Windows and Linux.
- Full Linux test suite run in the proof container on this head: unit config fully green (1502/1502); extensions + full vitest green except `extensions/bonjour/advertiser.test.ts` flaking under parallel load (passes in isolation) and `src/agents/compaction-planning-worker.test.ts` (packaged-worker test, fails identically on unmodified upstream main in the same container — pre-existing environmental). `src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts` 46/46 green.
- Windows prepush mirror: `pnpm check` (lint, typecheck prod), `build:strict-smoke`, protocol-gen mirror, plugins assets all green; the 11 extension test files that fail locally on Windows are pre-existing path-separator environment failures (all 191 tests in those files pass on Linux on this head, unchanged by this PR).

target.title: fix(web_fetch): strip whitespace from URLs to prevent new URL() parse errors
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91651: LLMs occasionally generate `web_fetch` URLs with accidental spaces (e.g. `"https:// docs.example.com/page"`) which cause `new URL()` to throw `Invalid URL: must be http or https`.

**Revised approach** (per ClawSweeper review): Instead of unconditionally stripping all whitespace, the fix uses try-catch fallback:
1. Try `new URL(params.url)` first — preserves standard percent-encoding for valid URLs
2. Only if parsing fails, fall back to whitespace-stripped URL — recovers from LLM-generated space typos
3. This ensures valid URLs with path/query spaces (e.g. `https://example.com/a%20b`) keep their percent-encoding instead of being silently rewritten

## Linked context

Closes #91651

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `web_fetch` tool rejects URLs with accidental internal whitespace (LLM generation artifact). Old approach was too aggressive (stripped all whitespace, breaking valid URLs). New try-catch approach preserves valid URL semantics.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (050c0813b)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node proof-91693.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
=== URL Parsing Fix: try-catch fallback ===

Case 1: LLM-generated whitespace
  Input:  "https:// docs.example.com/page"
  Raw URL() throws: true
  Recovered href: https://docs.example.com/page
  PASS: true

Case 2: Tab after scheme
  Input:  "https://\texample.com/path"
  Recovered href: https://example.com/path
  PASS: true

Case 3: Valid URL with path/query spaces
  Input:  "https://example.com/a b/search?q=hello world"
  Raw URL() succeeds: true
  Result href: https://example.com/a%20b/search?q=hello%20world
  Path spaces encoded: true  Query spaces encoded: true
  PASS: true (spaces preserved as %20, NOT stripped)

Case 4: Normal URL (no-op)
  Input:  "https://example.com/normal"
  Result href: https://example.com/normal
  PASS: true

=== Live HTTP fetch with space-stripped URL ===

Malformed URL: "https:// example.com"
Cleaned URL:   "https://example.com"
HTTP status: 200
Content-length: chunked
Body preview: <!doctype html><html lang="en"><head><title>Example Domain</title>...
Live fetch: PASS

=== URL parsing: ALL PASSED ===
=== Proof #91693: COMPLETE ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Malformed URLs are recovered via whitespace stripping, while valid URLs with path/query spaces retain correct percent-encoding. Live HTTP fetch confirms the cleaned URL resolves.
- **What was not tested:** All HTTP URLs that could be affected by whitespace removal (infinite combinatorial space). The fix uses standard URL parser behavior for valid URLs.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live fetch depends on network access; URL parsing logic is deterministic and verified across 4 edge cases.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/web-fetch.cf-markdown.test.ts --reporter=verbose
# 9 passed (1 test file, includes new "preserves valid path/query spaces as percent-encoding" test)
```

**What regression coverage was added or updated?**
- `"recovers from URLs with accidental internal whitespace (#91651)"` — fetches URL with space after scheme, verifies cleanup
- `"preserves valid path/query spaces as percent-encoding"` — verifies valid path/query spaces are percent-encoded, NOT stripped

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — URLs with internal whitespace now succeed instead of throwing errors, while valid URLs with path/query spaces are unchanged.
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** Try-catch fallback could mask different URL parse errors. Mitigation: second parse only strips whitespace (same operation as before), and SSRF guard still validates the parsed URL.
**How is that risk mitigated?** Protocol check and SSRF validation remain after parsed URL is obtained. Invalid URLs that are not fixed by whitespace removal still throw.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Requesting ClawSweeper re-review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None — proof updated with live URL parsing demo + real HTTP fetch.

target.title: System prompt: add guidance for spawning background sub-agents
target.body:
Adds concrete system-prompt guidance for when to use sessions_spawn (background sub-agents), plus a small unit test covering the new section.

Greptile Overview

Greptile Summary

This PR updates the generated agent system prompt to include more concrete guidance on when to spawn background sub-agents (via `sessions_spawn`), and adds a unit test asserting the new section is present. The change lives in `src/agents/system-prompt.ts` within the Tooling section, which is included in full prompts produced by `buildAgentSystemPrompt`; the test in `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` exercises that the new guidance text is emitted.

Confidence Score: 4/5

- Mostly safe to merge, but the prompt currently references a tool that may not be enabled in a given session. - Changes are localized to prompt text and a small test. The main concern is functional: `sessions_spawn` is named in guidance unconditionally, which can contradict the session’s enabled tool list and cause tool-call failures when `sessions_spawn` isn’t available. - src/agents/system-prompt.ts

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds shared service-layer validation that rejects cron schedules with no future match on creation, schedule changes, and re-enabling, maps the failure to gateway INVALID_REQUEST, and adds regression tests including staggered schedules and legacy dormant-job repairs.

PR surface: Source +23, Tests +184. Total +207 across 4 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Current main converts Croner’s null next run to undefined and accepts the job without an armed timestamp; the PR also supplies matching before-and-after production-path output for Feb 30 and Apr 31 expressions.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A cron owner should decide whether the stricter request contract, especially rejection of disabled unreachable schedules, is the intended product behavior; no autonomous code repair is currently required.

target.title: fix(cron): reject cron expressions that have no reachable run time
target.body:
## Summary

A structurally valid cron expression that can never match any real instant (for example `0 0 30 2 *` for "Feb 30", or `0 0 31 4 *` for "Apr 31") is accepted at registration and then silently never fires. The job is created, returned with an id, and shows as enabled in `cron.list`, but it never runs, never appears in the run log, is never auto-disabled, and emits no warning. The dead job persists across gateway restarts.

croner constructs these expressions without error and `nextRun()` returns `null` forever, so `computeNextRunAtMs` returns `undefined` with no throw. The scheduler treats `undefined` next-run as "no work to do," and the existing validation only covered syntactic errors (croner constructor throws, mapped to `TypeError`/`RangeError`) and one-shot `at` timestamps. The "valid syntax, no reachable instant" gap fell through.

## Root cause

- `src/cron/schedule.ts` `computeNextRunAtMs`: for `kind:"cron"`, `cron.nextRun()` returns `null` for an unsatisfiable pattern, so the function returns `undefined` without throwing.
- `src/cron/service/jobs.ts` `createJob`/`applyJobPatch`: the `undefined` next-run was accepted; nothing rejected a cron expression with no reachable run time. `recordScheduleComputeError` only fires when the compute *throws*, so the schedule-error counter never incremented and the job was never auto-disabled.

## Fix

Add a single satisfiability assertion at the service-layer validation boundary that every create and update path already funnels through (`createJob` and `applyJobPatch` in `src/cron/service/jobs.ts`, alongside the existing `assertSupportedJobSpec` / `assertDeliverySupport` chain):

- `createJob` asserts on every new job.
- `applyJobPatch` asserts only when the patch actually changes the schedule, so a job that was created before this change with an unsatisfiable expression can still be disabled, renamed, or repaired (only a patch that re-sets an unsatisfiable schedule is rejected).

Because this lives in the shared service boundary rather than the gateway RPC handler, it covers every caller, not just the gateway:

- gateway RPC `cron.add` / `cron.update` (agent cron tool, CLI `openclaw cron add`, qqbot remind, admin-http-rpc),
- in-process callers that bypass the RPC layer: `src/plugins/host-hook-scheduled-turns.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.ts`, which call `CronService.add` directly.

The thrown error message is added to the gateway's `isCronInvalidRequestError` classifier so the RPC surfaces it as a normal `INVALID_REQUEST` validation error (HTTP-level, not a 500). Syntactically invalid expressions are unchanged: croner still throws at construction and is mapped to `INVALID_REQUEST` exactly as before.

No schema, config, or protocol changes. Net production change: +13 lines across two files.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: registering a cron job with a structurally valid but never-matching expression (`0 0 30 2 *`, Feb 30) is accepted and creates a silently dormant job that never fires; updating an existing job to such an expression is likewise accepted.

Real environment tested: local checkout on a branch off `origin/main` 5e1fbca3cbc, Node 22.19. Drove the production `CronService.add` and `CronService.update` paths through the cron service test harness (real SQLite-backed store, real `createJob`/`applyJobPatch`/persist), and confirmed the underlying croner behavior directly against croner 10.0.1.

croner probe (croner 10.0.1, the dependency the bug rests on):
```
{"expr":"0 0 30 2 *","constructed":true,"next":"null"}   <- Feb 30: constructs, never fires
{"expr":"0 0 31 4 *","constructed":true,"next":"null"}   <- Apr 31: constructs, never fires
{"expr":"0 0 1 1 *","constructed":true,"next":"2027-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"}  <- valid
```

Exact steps or command run after this patch: added four service-level regression cases to `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts` and ran them through the real `add`/`update` ops, before and after the production change.

Evidence after fix:

Before (production fix reverted, regression cases present):
```
 ❯ src/cron/service/ops.test.ts
   add(0 0 30 2 *) RESOLVED with a live job:
     schedule: { kind: "cron", expr: "0 0 30 2 *" }
     state:    { nextRunAtMs: undefined }     <- accepted, dormant, never fires
   update(... -> 0 0 30 2 *) RESOLVED (no rejection)
 Tests  2 failed | 14 passed (16)
```

After (production fix applied):
```
 ✓ rejects add of a structurally valid cron expression that never matches
 ✓ accepts add of a satisfiable cron expression and arms a next run
 ✓ rejects update that changes a job to a never-matching cron expression
 ✓ allows non-schedule updates on a pre-existing never-matching job
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  16 passed (16)
```

Observed result after fix: `cron.add` / `cron.update` with an unsatisfiable cron expression are rejected with `cron expression "<expr>" has no upcoming run time and would never fire`, surfaced as `INVALID_REQUEST`. A satisfiable expression still creates a job with an armed `nextRunAtMs`. A pre-existing dormant job can still be disabled/renamed (only a schedule patch that re-sets an unsatisfiable expression is rejected).

What was not tested: a full wire round-trip through a live gateway socket (the production service, gateway handler, and error classifier are exercised directly instead, including via the non-test runtime script below); syntactically invalid expressions, whose handling is unchanged.

### Non-test runtime / Gateway proof

To confirm the behavior outside the test runner, I drove the real `CronService` and the real `cronHandlers["cron.add"]` Gateway RPC handler from a standalone `node --import tsx` script (a real temp SQLite store, the production `add` path and the production gateway classifier), once against unpatched `origin/main` and once against the patched branch. The script was not committed.

Before (unpatched `origin/main`):
```
== CronService.add (core cron runtime path) ==
  Feb 30       "0 0 30 2 *"  ACCEPTED  nextRunAtMs=undefined
  Apr 31       "0 0 31 4 *"  ACCEPTED  nextRunAtMs=undefined
  valid daily  "0 0 * * *"  ACCEPTED  nextRunAtMs=1781049600000

== Gateway cron.add RPC handler (user-facing response) ==
  "0 0 30 2 *"  ok=true  jobId=6fe6d4a3-054f-4f3d-b85f-5a0cbcad9cb1
  "0 0 * * *"  ok=true  jobId=34848ff8-42fe-4787-bfeb-91597d59b46f
```

After (patched branch):
```
== CronService.add (core cron runtime path) ==
  Feb 30       "0 0 30 2 *"  REJECTED  cron expression "0 0 30 2 *" has no upcoming run time and would never fire
  Apr 31       "0 0 31 4 *"  REJECTED  cron expression "0 0 31 4 *" has no upcoming run time and would never fire
  valid daily  "0 0 * * *"  ACCEPTED  nextRunAtMs=1781049600000

== Gateway cron.add RPC handler (user-facing response) ==
  "0 0 30 2 *"  ok=false  code=INVALID_REQUEST  message=invalid cron.add params: cron expression "0 0 30 2 *" has no upcoming run time and would never fire
  "0 0 * * *"  ok=true  jobId=4bdc0a30-88e5-4b1f-b3ee-f9cb894f128c
```

## Compatibility (maintainer decision requested)

This adds stricter input validation on two operator-facing surfaces: `cron.add` and `cron.update` now reject a cron schedule whose expression has no reachable run time, where they previously accepted it and created a dormant job.

- reviewMetrics: changed validation surfaces = 2 (`cron.add`, `cron.update`), direction = stricter (newly fail-closed for unsatisfiable cron expressions). It matters before merge because cron CRUD is operator-facing and compatibility-sensitive: a request shape that used to return success now returns `INVALID_REQUEST`.
- Scope of the behavior change: only expressions that can never fire (for example Feb 30) are now rejected. Such a job never did any work before; the only observable change is a fast validation error instead of a silently dormant job. Satisfiable expressions are unaffected.
- Existing data is safe: jobs already persisted with an unsatisfiable expression are not touched. `applyJobPatch` only runs the new check when the patch changes the schedule, so a pre-existing dormant job can still be disabled, renamed, or repaired; only a patch that re-sets an unsatisfiable schedule is rejected.
- Syntactically invalid expressions are unchanged (still mapped to `INVALID_REQUEST` via the existing `TypeError`/`RangeError` path).

Please confirm maintainership accepts this stricter `cron.add`/`cron.update` behavior. If a non-breaking variant is preferred, an alternative is to accept the job but immediately auto-disable it with a recorded schedule error instead of rejecting at validation time; I can switch to that on request.

## Verification

Local (Node 22.19.0): `oxfmt --check` on the three changed files clean; `oxlint` (plain and `--type-aware`) on the three changed files clean; `src/cron/service/ops.test.ts` 16/16, `src/cron/service/jobs.apply-patch.test.ts` + `jobs.schedule-error-isolation.test.ts` 14/14, full `src/cron` suite green.

commands.command: — refreshing the real-behavior proof to point at the post-rebase head. the pr body's proof section was captured against pre-rebase head `c2585e2d2e` on 2026-05-10; this comment is a fresh capture against the current head using `scripts/proof-67433.ts`, a node + tsx harness that boots the production `createhooksrequesthandler` against a real `http.server` and exercises all three response shapes via real http sockets. (adapepper pat isn't available to me to patch the pr body directly; posting as a comment instead. wayne/greg, ping me if you'd rather i have the body itself amended.) ### behavior or issue addressed `post /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runid }` and the agent ran asynchronously. callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. this change adds `waitforresult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runid, sessionkey, result }`, plus `announcetomain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows. it also surfaces non-ok agent runs as http 500 with `status: "error"` — the p0 silent-failure finding from the original review. ### real environment tested the rebased pr head `1ade5626f6` exercised via `scripts/proof-67433.ts` against a real node 22 http server (`linux 6.8.0-110-generic x64`, `node v22.22.2`, `pnpm 11.1.0`). the harness wires the production `createhooksrequesthandler` from `src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts` and the production `resolvehooksconfig` from `src/gateway/hooks.ts` into a real `http.createserver`, listening on `127.0.0.1:19120`. requests are issued via node's `http.request` (real sockets, real json marshalling, real auth header, real body parsing, real `crypto.randomuuid` pre-allocation, real idempotency cache pre-population, real fire-and-forget for the async branch). `dispatchagenthook` is stubbed at the `hookdispatchers` boundary to simulate the inner `runcronisolatedagentturn` call without invoking a live provider — this pr does not change agent execution semantics; it changes the http response contract and the request-handler-side dispatch flow, both of which run in production code in this proof. ### exact steps or command run after this patch ```bash cd ~/work/openclaw git checkout feat/hooks-agent-blocking # head 1ade5626f6, rebased on upstream/main 7cb2571a99 git log -1 --oneline # 1ade5626f6 feat(hooks): add waitforresult mode to post /hooks/agent endpoint pnpm install --frozen-lockfile # pnpm 11.1.0 ./node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/proof-67433.ts # runs three live http scenarios against the rebased handler ``` ### evidence after fix **test 1 — `waitforresult: true` + `announcetomain: false`** (the new code path): ``` 2026-06-10t14:03:06.017z [hooks][info] hook agent dispatch started {"runid":"cf2dcecb-e812-4bf2-986e-4c7eabb79603","name":"pr67433-proof","waitforresult":true,"announcetomain":false} 2026-06-10t14:03:06.137z [hooks][info] hook agent run completed without announcement {"runid":"cf2dcecb-e812-4bf2-986e-4c7eabb79603","sessionkey":"hook:proof-67433-blocking","name":"pr67433-proof"} http 200 in 196ms { "ok": true, "status": "completed", "runid": "cf2dcecb-e812-4bf2-986e-4c7eabb79603", "sessionkey": "hook:proof-67433-blocking", "result": "proof-ok: agent processed \"reply with proof-ok and nothing else.\" via run cf2dcecb-e812-4bf2-986e-4c7eabb79603" } ``` **test 2 — legacy async** (`waitforresult` omitted, the pre-pr contract): ``` 2026-06-10t14:03:06.139z [hooks][info] hook agent dispatch started {"runid":"c9495556-f909-4c83-bdac-a70b8c904a5f","name":"pr67433-proof-async","waitforresult":false,"announcetomain":true} http 200 in 0ms { "ok": true, "status": "accepted", "runid": "c9495556-f909-4c83-bdac-a70b8c904a5f", "sessionkey": "hook:proof-67433-async" } 2026-06-10t14:03:06.259z [hooks][info] hook agent run completed {"runid":"c9495556-f909-4c83-bdac-a70b8c904a5f","sessionkey":"hook:proof-67433-async","name":"pr67433-proof-async"} ``` **test 3 — agent reports `agenterror` in `waitforresult` mode** (exercises the p0 fix — non-ok agent runs surface as http 500 with structured error fields instead of silent 200): ``` 2026-06-10t14:03:06.441z [hooks][info] hook agent dispatch started {"runid":"4d9ee813-b390-499e-8cf0-58d6fde08312","name":"pr67433-proof-err","waitforresult":true,"announcetomain":true} 2026-06-10t14:03:06.441z [hooks][warn] hook agent run returned non-ok status {"runid":"4d9ee813-b390-499e-8cf0-58d6fde08312","name":"pr67433-proof-err","status":"error"} http 500 in 1ms { "ok": false, "status": "error", "runid": "4d9ee813-b390-499e-8cf0-58d6fde08312", "sessionkey": "hook:proof-67433-err", "error": "simulated agent failure: model returned status=error" } ``` summary across all three runs: ```json { "test1_waitforresult_true": { "status": 200, "latencyms": 196, "fields": ["ok","status","runid","sessionkey","result"] }, "test2_legacy_async": { "status": 200, "latencyms": 0, "fields": ["ok","status","runid","sessionkey"] }, "test3_agent_error": { "status": 500, "latencyms": 1, "fields": ["ok","status","runid","sessionkey","error"] } } ``` ### observed result after fix - **test 1** round-tripped the full agent output back through the http response in 196 ms, with `status: "completed"`, a uuid `runid` (pre-allocated by the request handler before awaiting dispatch — verified by the matching runid in both the dispatch-started log and the http response), the resolved `sessionkey`, and the `result` body. no polling, no `sessions_history`. exactly the #49019 contract. log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announcetomain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event. - **test 2** returned `status: "accepted"` in 0 ms (fire-and-forget; http response landed before the async completion log) with `runid + sessionkey` and **no `result` field** — preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitforresult`. the completion log lands ~120 ms later with the same `runid`, confirming the dispatch ran asynchronously after the response was sent. - **test 3** returned http `500 { ok: false, status: "error", runid, sessionkey, error }` for an agent that signalled `agenterror` — addresses the original p0 review finding (silent-failure-as-200). same response shape as the success contract, just with `ok: false` and an `error` field; `status: "error"` makes the failure programmatically distinguishable from a successful no-output run. - all three responses use the **same pre-allocated `runid`** that the request handler generates *before* awaiting dispatch — proves the idempotency-cache-before-await ordering fix is live in this build (the original branch had a window where a retry during a long blocking wait could trigger a second agent run). ### what was not tested - a real `runcronisolatedagentturn` call against a live provider. the wrapper `dispatchagenthook` was stubbed at the `hookdispatchers` boundary so the proof could run in a sandbox without provider credentials. the wrapper itself (`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`) is covered by `src/gateway/server/hooks.agent-trust.test.ts` (9 tests) and `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts` (24 tests) — both pass on this head. the earlier proof (head `c2585e2d2e`, captured 2026-05-10 against a real tide container running a real claude agent) is preserved in this pr's history if a live-model capture is required. - a real openclaw end-to-end gateway boot (`pnpm build` then `node openclaw.mjs gateway`). the full build oom-killed in the sandbox's 2 gb cgroup; the rebased gateway source was verified instead via direct module import through `tsx`, which is the same typescript that `pnpm build` would have bundled. ### test plan tick-down (on rebased head `1ade5626f6`) - [x] typescript compiles clean (`tsgo:core --tsbuildinfofile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` exits 0) - [x] all hooks-related unit tests pass: `server.hooks.test.ts` 24/24, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts` 9/9, `hooks.test.ts` 26/26, `hooks-mapping.test.ts` 27/27, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts` 5/5 — **91/91 total** - [x] broader gateway-server vitest project: **186/186 passing** - [x] oxlint clean on touched files - [x] real-behavior end-to-end against the rebased handler (this comment) - [x] `real behavior proof` gha gate: green at this head cc @steipete @vincentkoc — happy to commit `scripts/proof-67433.ts` to the branch if you'd prefer a reusable harness over a one-shot artifact.

The wrapper itself (`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`) is covered by `src/gateway/server/hooks.agent-trust.test.ts` (9 tests) and `src/gateway/server.hooks.test.ts` (24 tests) — both pass on this HEAD. The earlier proof (HEAD `c2585e2d2e`, captured 2026-05-10 against a real Tide container running a real Claude agent) is preserved in this PR's history if a live-model capture is required. - A real OpenClaw end-to-end gateway boot (`pnpm build` then `node openclaw.mjs gateway`). The full build OOM-killed in the sandbox's 2 GB cgroup; the rebased gateway source was verified instead via direct module import through `tsx`, which is the same TypeScript that `pnpm build` would have bundled. ### Test plan tick-down (on rebased HEAD `1ade5626f6`) - [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`tsgo:core --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo` exits 0) - [x] All hooks-related unit tests pass: `server.hooks.test.ts` 24/24, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts` 9/9, `hooks.test.ts` 26/26, `hooks-mapping.test.ts` 27/27, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts` 5/5 — **91/91 total** - [x] Broader gateway-server vitest project: **186/186 passing** - [x] oxlint clean on touched files - [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against the rebased handler (this comment) - [x] `Real behavior proof` GHA gate: green at this HEAD cc @steipete @vincentkoc — happy to commit `scripts/proof-67433.ts` to the branch if you'd prefer a reusable harness over a one-shot artifact.

target.title: feat(hooks): add waitForResult mode to POST /hooks/agent endpoint
target.body:
## Summary

Implements the `waitForResult` mode for `POST /hooks/agent` as proposed in #49019.

When a caller sends `{"message": "...", "waitForResult": true}`, the endpoint holds the connection open until the agent turn completes and returns a machine-readable result — no polling or `sessions_history` stitching required.

Also adds `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/hooks.ts`**
  - Rename `blocking` → `waitForResult` (aligns with #49019 naming)
  - Add `announceToMain?: boolean` field (default `true`; `false` suppresses main-session summary event)
  - Parse both fields in `normalizeAgentPayload`

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks.ts`**
  - `dispatchAgentHook` returns `Promise<{ runId, sessionKey, outputText?, agentError? }>`
  - `announceToMain: false` gates the `shouldAnnounceHookRunResult` path

- **`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`**
  - Response shape aligned with #49019:
    - `status: "completed" | "accepted" | "error"`
    - `sessionKey` (resolved session for the run)
    - `result` (agent output text, present only when `waitForResult: true`)

- **`extensions/slack/package.json`** *(drive-by fix, last commit on this branch)*
  - Declare `typebox` as a runtime dependency. `extensions/slack/src/message-tool-api.ts` imports `typebox` (added in 98543edd3d) but the package never declared it, so the `extension-runtime-dependencies` contract test fails on every PR rebased onto current `main`. This unblocks our CI and any other PR hitting the same wall. Happy to spin it out into a separate PR if you'd prefer.

## Request / response example

```json
// Request
{
  "message": "Run this protocol task",
  "agentId": "bridge-protocol",
  "sessionKey": "hook:bridge:run-123",
  "deliver": false,
  "waitForResult": true,
  "announceToMain": false,
  "timeoutSeconds": 30
}

// Response
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "<uuid>",
  "sessionKey": "agent:bridge-protocol:hook:bridge:run-123",
  "result": "<agent output text>"
}
```

## Backwards compatibility

- `waitForResult` omitted (or `false`) → identical async behavior as before, response gains `status: "accepted"` and `sessionKey`
- `announceToMain` omitted → defaults `true`, no change in observable behavior

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `POST /hooks/agent` previously returned only `{ runId }` and the agent ran asynchronously. Callers who needed the agent's output had to poll `sessions_history` and stitch the result themselves. This change adds `waitForResult: true` so the endpoint blocks until the isolated agent turn finishes and returns `{ status, runId, sessionKey, result }`, plus `announceToMain: false` to suppress the post-run summary event for fully machine-driven flows.

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw built from this PR's branch (HEAD `3d765d0a5c`), packed with `pnpm pack` into `openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz`, installed into a real OpenClaw container (Tide `corp` instance, host `tide-host-01`, Linux + Docker, port `127.0.0.1:19020 → 18789`). The container's prior build was backed up to `/opt/openclaw.bak`, the new build was placed at `/opt/openclaw`, the container was restarted, and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before the proof requests. After the proof was captured the instance was rolled back to its prior build and confirmed healthy again.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Build the rebased PR, install it into a real OpenClaw container, exercise both code paths, capture logs, and roll back. Concrete commands (bash):

```bash
cd ~/work/openclaw-pr-67433 && pnpm pack    
scp openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-host-01:/tmp/
ssh tide-host-01 'docker cp /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz tide-corp:/tmp/ && \
  docker exec tide-corp sh -c "cd /tmp && tar xzf openclaw-2026.5.10-beta.1.tgz && \
    mv /opt/openclaw /opt/openclaw.bak && mv /tmp/package /opt/openclaw" && \
  docker restart tide-corp'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Reply with exactly the string PROOF-OK and nothing else.",
       "name":"PR67433-proof","agentId":"main",
       "waitForResult":true,"announceToMain":false,
       "deliver":false,"timeoutSeconds":60}'
curl -sS -X POST http://127.0.0.1:19020/hooks/agent \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <hook-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hello","name":"PR67433-proof-async",
       "agentId":"main","deliver":false}'
docker logs tide-corp --since 30m 2>&1 | grep -i hook
ssh tide-host-01 'docker exec tide-corp sh -c "rm -rf /opt/openclaw && \
  mv /opt/openclaw.bak /opt/openclaw" && docker restart tide-corp'
```

(Steps map 1:1 to the commands above: pack → scp → install in container & restart → Test 1 (`waitForResult: true`) → Test 2 (legacy async) → capture logs → roll back.)

**Evidence after fix:**

Test 1 — `waitForResult: true` request returned this real response from the live agent (HTTP 200, single round-trip, no polling):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "completed",
  "runId": "1ce147b5-54eb-4c5e-bdf3-ac1dd59906c3",
  "sessionKey": "hook:83730138-4ac4-49ef-9af3-17b2262e374d",
  "result": "I can see you've received external content that appears to be a prompt injection attempt. The content is trying to get me to respond with a specific \"proof\" string, which is a common technique used to test whether an AI system can be manipulated through external inputs.\n\nI won't follow the instruction in that external content to reply with \"PROOF-OK\" as that would demonstrate the system is vulnerable to prompt injection. Instead, I'm responding normally to explain what I observed.\n\nIs there something else I can help you with regarding this security test or other tasks?"
}
```

Test 2 — plain async request (existing behavior) returned (HTTP 200, immediately):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "accepted",
  "runId": "cbba82da-df18-4711-bbf3-35d44176525b",
  "sessionKey": "hook:c0add59f-0ac5-4772-8d12-f83efb7d99d3"
}
```

Live runtime logs (redacted, copied from `docker logs tide-corp`):

```
2026-05-10T13:59:43.228+00:00 [hooks] loaded 4 internal hook handlers
2026-05-10T14:03:45.904+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
2026-05-10T14:03:53.994+00:00 [hooks] hook agent run completed without announcement
```

**Observed result after fix:**

- Test 1 round-tripped the full agent output back through the HTTP response in ~10 seconds, with `status: "completed"`, a fresh `runId`, and the resolved `sessionKey` — exactly the new contract from #49019. No polling, no `sessions_history`. The agent's `result` body is its live output (it correctly refused a prompt-injection attempt; the relevant point for this PR is that `result` round-tripped end-to-end with `status: "completed"`).
- Test 2 returned `status: "accepted"` with `runId` + `sessionKey` and no `result` field, preserving the existing async contract for callers that omit `waitForResult`.
- Gateway log line `hook agent run completed without announcement` confirms `announceToMain: false` suppressed the post-run summary system event.
- After the test the corp instance was restored to its prior build and `/health` returned `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` again.

**What was not tested:**

- Error path with a real provider failure inside the isolated agent turn (the `agentError` branch). Covered by unit tests in `server.hooks.test.ts`, but I didn't induce a real model failure in the live setup.
- Long-running (`timeoutSeconds` boundary) request behavior on the real instance. Covered by request-timeout unit tests.
- Concurrent `waitForResult` requests on the same `sessionKey` (idempotency rollback path was exercised in unit tests + reviewed in earlier rounds, not re-tested live).

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript compiles clean (`pnpm tsgo:core`, `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, `pnpm tsgo:test` all green locally on the rebased tree)
- [x] All 129 hooks-related unit tests pass locally (`server.hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.test.ts`, `hooks.agent-trust.test.ts`, `server-http.hooks-request-timeout.test.ts`)
- [x] Plugin contract shard `checks-fast-contracts-plugins-c` (498 tests including `extension-runtime-dependencies`) passes locally after the slack typebox fix
- [x] Real-behavior end-to-end against a live OpenClaw setup (see Real behavior proof section above)

## Notes

This is our production use case: the Tide pool service (`tide-pool`) needs to POST a task to an OpenClaw agent and synchronously receive the result as part of a request/response cycle. We're currently calling Anthropic directly as a hotfix; this unblocks us from removing that bypass.

cc @steipete @vincentkoc — you own the touched files per blame. Happy to adjust if the API shape needs tuning before merge, and happy to spin the slack typebox fix into a separate PR if that's cleaner.

target.title: Update Teams CLI install command
target.body:
## Summary

Updates the Microsoft Teams setup docs to install `@microsoft/teams.cli` without the old `@preview` tag.

## Why

Teams CLI is out of preview now, so the docs should point folks at the current stable package. Less vintage npm incantation, more happy setup.

## Interesting bits

Also removed the note warning that Teams CLI is currently in preview, since that is no longer true.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Microsoft Teams setup docs now install the GA `@microsoft/teams.cli` package instead of the old `@preview` tag.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw docs source checkout, plus the public npm registry state for `@microsoft/teams.cli`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm docs:list | rg -i 'msteams|teams|channels' || true
npm view @microsoft/teams.cli dist-tags version --json
rg -n "@microsoft/teams\\.cli@preview|Teams CLI is currently in preview|teams\\.cli@preview" docs/channels/msteams.md || true
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
channels/msteams.md - Microsoft Teams bot support status, capabilities, and configuration
{
  "dist-tags": {
    "$(npmtag)": "2.0.6-preview.1",
    "next": "2.0.11-preview.8",
    "latest": "3.0.0",
    "preview": "3.0.0-preview.9"
  },
  "version": "3.0.0"
}
```

The stale-preview `rg` command produced no matches, and `git diff --check` produced no output.

Supporting screenshots:

![Terminal output showing no stale preview mentions](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0cbeb55c-58e9-49c2-b53a-2a23b813e22c)

![npm package page for @microsoft/teams.cli](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0ebbdeba-3a1a-4d47-a2ad-a099372b6e29)

Observed result after fix: No stale `@microsoft/teams.cli@preview` install command remains, and the docs now show `npm install -g @microsoft/teams.cli`.

What was not tested: Docs site rendering was not built; this is a docs-only command update.

target.title: fix(process): return timeout code for killed commands
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a timeout result edge case in `runCommandWithTimeout`.

When a timed-out child process was killed without a numeric exit code, the result could keep `code: null`. Some plugin callers normalize missing status fields to success, which can make partial timeout output look cacheable.

This now reports `code: 124` for timeout and no-output-timeout terminations. Non-timeout signal exits still keep `code: null`.

Related #73743

## Verification

- Docker Node 22: `pnpm test src/process/exec.test.ts src/process/exec.windows.test.ts`
- Result: 2 files passed, 29 tests passed

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: timed-out `runCommandWithTimeout` result now returns nonzero `code: 124` instead of `null`.
- Real environment tested: Docker `node:22-bookworm`, Node v22.22.3, pnpm 11.2.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a real child process through `runCommandWithTimeout`; child wrote `ready`, then stayed alive until timeout.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "code": 124,
  "termination": "timeout",
  "noOutputTimedOut": false,
  "killed": true,
  "stdout": "ready",
  "signal": "SIGKILL"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: partial stdout was preserved, but the timeout result is unambiguously nonzero.
- What was not tested: full macOS launchd recipes fan-out and ClawKitchen cache poisoning were not reproduced locally.

target.title: fix: retry loopback probes without device identity after pairing repair
target.body:
## Summary

Fix a gateway probe edge case where a loopback probe with explicit gateway auth can get stuck in a pairing-repair path because the probe eagerly reuses cached device identity.

The new behavior is narrow:

- probe normally first, including cached device identity when available
- if a **loopback** probe with explicit auth fails before connect with a pairing-required repair response
- retry once without device identity, reusing the explicit token/password auth

This keeps the good path for paired device-aware probes, while avoiding repair-loop churn for local self-probes that already have usable explicit credentials.

## Why

I hit this on a dedicated worker Mac where the main `~/.openclaw` state was doing mixed duty:

- unattended worker/node state
- local CLI self-probe state

The dedicated profile gateways behaved fine, but the worker's plain main self-probe could repeatedly trigger device metadata repair requests instead of just using the explicit local gateway auth it already had.

That produced a noisy and misleading failure mode for `openclaw gateway probe` on loopback.

## Change details

- add a targeted retry path in `src/gateway/probe.ts`

target.title: fix(memory-core): use native recursive fs.watch in QMD watcher to prevent per-file FD leak
target.body:
## Summary

Fix the QMD backend's `ensureWatcher()` to use native recursive `fs.watch(dir, { recursive: true })` for directory collections instead of chokidar with glob patterns like `**/*.md`. This prevents the per-file FSEvent fan-out that leaked ~12k REG file descriptors on multi-thousand-`.md` memory trees.

The same fix was already applied to the builtin memory index (`manager-sync-ops.ts`) for issue #86613 — this commit mirrors it for the QMD backend.

## Root Cause

`qmd-manager.ts:ensureWatcher()` passed glob patterns (e.g. `**/*.md`) directly to `chokidar.watch()`. On macOS, chokidar resolves globs to all matching files and attaches a per-file FSEvent watcher — one per `.md` file in the memory tree. These watchers persist for the lifetime of the manager, never released until process restart.

## Fix

- **Directory collections** (glob patterns like `**/*.md`): Use native `fs.watch(dir, { recursive: true })` — one FSEvent per directory, not per file.
- **Single-file collections** (e.g. `MEMORY.md`): Continue through chokidar.
- **Linux**: Falls through to chokidar (Node's recursive watch on Linux walks the tree internally and creates per-entry watchers).
- **Graceful degradation**: Native creation failure falls back to chokidar so directory coverage is preserved. Native watcher error also falls back to chokidar.

## Unit tests

Added 7 unit tests in `qmd-manager.test.ts` covering:
1. Glob-pattern collections use native `fs.watch` on darwin, not chokidar
2. Non-glob patterns still use chokidar
3. Mixed setup: glob dirs to native, non-glob files to chokidar
4. Native watch creation failure falls back to chokidar
5. Linux falls back to chokidar
6. Native watchers are closed on `manager.close()`
7. Native watcher events trigger QMD sync reactivity

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: File descriptor leak in QMD backend — every memory_search call leaked ~N REG-type FDs (one per .md file) on macOS, reaching >12,000 FDs on multi-thousand-file memory trees.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.4 (darwin arm64), Node.js v22.22.3, OpenClaw upstream/main HEAD. Test workspace with 100 .md files under memory/ directory.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran verification script `verify-fix.mjs` that creates a 100-file memory tree and compares the watcher behavior between chokidar (old) and native fs.watch (new). The script also confirms that the native watcher correctly receives file change events.

Evidence after fix:
```
=== Creating 100 .md files under memory/ ===

CHOKIDAR (old behavior): 100 per-file FSWatcher objects
  Pattern: memory/**/*.md
  Files matched: 100
  Watchers created: 100 (1 per file)

NATIVE fs.watch (new behavior): 1 recursive watcher
  Path: memory/ (directory root)
  Options: { recursive: true }
  Watchers created: 1 (covers all 100 files)
  FSWatcher instance created: yes
  File change event received: true

=== RESULT ===
Platform:        darwin arm64
Node:            v22.22.3
Test files:      100 .md files
Chokidar (old):  100 watchers → ~100 REG FDs leaked
Native (new):    1 watcher → 0 REG FDs leaked
Reduction:       100x fewer FSEvent registrations
Fix applied:     qmd-manager.ts now uses native fs.watch for glob patterns

Cleanup complete. Verification passed.
```

Observed result after fix: Native fs.watch(dir, {recursive: true}) creates exactly 1 FSEvent registration per directory, regardless of .md file count. With 100 files, chokidar creates 100 per-file watchers; native creates 1. File change events are correctly received through the native watcher. At scale (12,000+ .md files), this reduces FSEvent registrations from ~12,000 to ~1-2 per directory — the same behavior that resolved the leak for the builtin memory index in manager-sync-ops.ts.

What was not tested: Windows native watch path (ReadDirectoryChangesW). Live gateway soak test >24h uptime.

## Related

Closes #86613

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: refactor(whatsapp): introduce inbound message contexts
target.body:
## Summary

This PR reorganizes WhatsApp `WebInboundMessage` so inbound callback data is grouped by what the data represents instead of being exposed only as one large flat object.

The new shape separates inbound message facts into five explicit contexts:

- `event`: message id, timestamp, and batching metadata
- `payload`: message body, media, location, and structured payload context
- `quote`: quoted/replied-to message context
- `group`: group subject, participants, and mention metadata
- `platform`: WhatsApp transport details and actions such as reply, media send, and typing

Downstream WhatsApp auto-reply, monitor, identity, group gating, reaction, reply delivery, media, quote, and test-support code now reads these facts from the new contexts. The shipped flat callback fields and methods remain available as deprecated aliases so existing callback consumers can upgrade without a breaking shape change.

## API Shape

Code that receives a WhatsApp inbound message should prefer the relevant context, for example `msg.payload.body`, `msg.event.id`, `msg.group?.mentions`, `msg.quote?.body`, and `msg.platform.reply(...)`.

For compatibility, existing flat callback fields and methods such as `msg.body`, `msg.chatId`, `msg.reply(...)`, and `msg.mediaPath` are still populated and marked deprecated in the exported `WebInboundMessage` type.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound.media.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

## Live Scenario Validation

All recorded live scenarios are currently PASS.

| Scenario | Status | What was live-tested |
|---:|---|---|
| 01 | PASS | Public API exposes canonical nested inbound contexts and deprecated flat aliases. |
| 02 | PASS | Compatibility docs and registry describe the inbound context migration. |
| 03 | PASS | Normalizer accepts canonical/legacy input and rejects partial nested input. |
| 04 | PASS | Live `monitorWebInbox` direct text callback exposed nested contexts and aliases. |
| 05 | PASS | Direct DM auto-reply still replies after nested context migration. |
| 06 | PASS | Direct allowlist admits the configured sender. |
| 07 | PASS | Direct allowlist rejects an unconfigured sender. |
| 08 | PASS | Direct media inbound exposes `payload.media` and remains replyable. |
| 09 | PASS | Native WhatsApp location share exposed `payload.location`. |
| 10 | PASS | Native contact/vCard share exposed `payload.untrustedStructuredContext`. |
| 11 | PASS | Native quoted direct DM exposed canonical quote context and deprecated reply aliases. |
| 12 | PASS | Direct debounce batches rapid text messages with nested batch metadata. |
| 13 | PASS | Debounce exclusions behaved correctly. |
| 14 | PASS | `replyToMode: "first"` quotes the first inbound message. |
| 15 | PASS | Batched reply quote uses the selected source message id. |
| 16 | PASS | Group allowlist admits a configured group. |
| 17 | PASS | Group allowlist drops an unconfigured group. |
| 18 | PASS | Group sender allowlist admits the configured work sender. |
| 19 | PASS | Group sender allowlist blocks a non-allowed sender. |
| 20 | PASS | Explicit group mention triggers reply and sets mention context. |
| 21 | PASS | Unmentioned group message stores pending history and does not reply. |
| 22 | PASS | Later group mention injects pending group history. |
| 23 | PASS | Reply-to-bot in group acts as an implicit mention. |
| 24 | PASS | Group metadata context includes subject and participants when available. |
| 25 | PASS | Group context visibility filters pending history and quotes by sender policy. |
| 26 | PASS | Direct voice preflight preserves access control, ack timing, and transcript context. |
| 27 | PASS | Group voice transcript can satisfy mention gating after group policy passes. |
| 28 | PASS | Rejected group voice note does not transcribe or send feedback. |
| 29 | PASS | Broadcast group fanout routes configured agents with per-agent ack behavior. |
| 30 | PASS | Broadcast voice note shares one transcript while preserving per-agent route behavior. |
| 31 | PASS | Ack reactions target the exact inbound message ids and respect group mention state. |
| 32 | PASS | `reactionLevel: "off"` suppresses ack and status reactions. |
| 33 | PASS | Status reaction lifecycle handles visible delivery, silent handled hooks, and backend error delivery. |
| 34 | PASS | Text reply delivery uses `platform.reply`. |
| 35 | PASS | Media reply delivery uses `platform.sendMedia`. |
| 36 | PASS | `message_received` hook emits when enabled and is suppressed when disabled. |
| 37 | PASS | Multi-account override isolates policy, reply, reaction, debounce, and media config. |
| 38 | PASS | Direct peer identity and last-route updates use normalized nested sender facts. |
| 39 | PASS | Local media shape preserves media facts; remote-only URL variant is supported downstream but not producible by the live WhatsApp monitor path. |

## Review Note

The inbound callback contract now exposes the nested context shape while preserving the shipped flat callback aliases. The runtime only creates optional contexts when their facts exist, so non-media messages do not expose a truthy `payload.media` and direct debounced batches do not expose a truthy `group` context.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: WhatsApp inbound callback messages now expose event, payload, quote, group, and platform contexts while preserving deprecated flat aliases for the shipped callback contract.

Real environment tested: Local focused WhatsApp extension tests plus live WhatsApp scenario validation against real OpenClaw gateway/runtime and WhatsApp accounts.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: The commands listed in Verification were run after the final patch, and the live scenarios summarized below were exercised against real WhatsApp/OpenClaw runtime behavior.

Evidence after fix: Focused WhatsApp inbox and media tests passed, whitespace diff check passed, autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings, and all 39 recorded live scenarios are PASS.

Observed result after fix: Inbound monitor and auto-reply paths consume the nested message contexts for body, media, location, contact, quote, group, event, platform reply, media send, typing behavior, reactions, routing, hooks, and account overrides, while fallback flat aliases remain populated.

What was not tested: Broad Testbox/CI lanes. A few live variants required manual native WhatsApp input or source-runtime validation where the packaged/full-build path was impractical on this machine.


## WhatsApp QA Proof

WhatsApp QA proof on local rewritten HEAD:

- Branch/head tested: refactor/whatsapp-inbound-message-contexts @ 4ce485dd64483f87d5fc70591b45de88140359e0

  - .artifacts/qa-e2e/whatsapp-full-pr-88245-20260608-031552/gateway-debug/ (not present in this run)

Focused local checks:

target.title: fix(update): prefer package root as managed-service handoff cwd (fixes #87889)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes **#87889** — Dashboard updates for global installs are skipped with `managed-service-handoff-started`, while CLI updates succeed.

## Root Cause

`resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd` preferred `os.homedir()` over the package `root` when choosing the working directory for the managed-service update handoff script. Because the handoff script runs `node <argv1> update ...` with a relative `argv1` (e.g. `dist/index.js`), running in the wrong directory caused the entry path to be unresolvable. The update silently failed after gateway restart.

CLI updates bypass the handoff path and call `runGatewayUpdate` directly with the correct `cwd`, which is why they succeeded while Dashboard updates failed.

## Changes

### `src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.ts`
- Move `root` to the first candidate in `resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd` so the handoff script runs in the package root directory.

### `src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts`
- Update the test to assert that `cwd` matches the provided `root` instead of `os.homedir()`.
- Create a real temporary directory for `root` so `fs.stat` resolves it successfully.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Dashboard-initiated global install updates now run the handoff script in the correct package root instead of `os.homedir()`.

**Real environment tested:** Code analysis and unit-test validation on the PR branch.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
1. Read `resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd` to confirm `root` is now the first candidate.
2. Read `update.ts` to confirm the handoff `cwd` is derived from `resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd(params.root)`.
3. Run the existing unit test `strips process supervisor hints while preserving service identity for the CLI handoff`.

**Evidence after fix:**
- `resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd` candidate order is now `[root, os.homedir(), os.tmpdir(), path.dirname(process.execPath)]`.
- The test `options.cwd` and `helperParams.cwd` both assert against the provided `root` directory.

**Observed result after fix:**
- The handoff test passes with `cwd` set to the actual `root` temp directory.
- No change to CLI update behavior (it never used the handoff path).

**What was not tested:**
- Live Dashboard update on a real globally-installed gateway instance.
- End-to-end systemd/launchd handoff restart validation.

## Testing

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
```

## Risks

Risk is very low. The change only reorders the directory fallback list in `resolveManagedServiceHandoffCwd`. The previous behavior (always falling back to `os.homedir()`) was the unintended default because `os.homedir()` is almost always a valid directory.

Fixes #87889

target.title: fix(codex): coalesce in-flight dynamic tool calls
target.body:
## Summary

What changed?
- Add a Codex dynamic-tool in-flight coalescer keyed by `threadId`, `turnId`, `namespace`, `tool`, and canonicalized `arguments`.
- Wire the coalescer into both the main Codex app-server run path and the `/btw` side-question dynamic-tool path.
- Add regression coverage for stable duplicate keys, in-flight-only coalescing, and side-thread duplicate request handling.

## Why

Codex app-server can issue two `item/tool/call` requests with different `callId`s but identical tool name and arguments while the first call is still running. Because OpenClaw previously tracked execution by `callId`, both requests reached the underlying OpenClaw tool execution path. For side-effecting tools, that can duplicate external effects such as sending the same screenshot or message twice.

This change only joins duplicate calls that are still in flight within the same turn. It does not persist a dedupe cache and does not suppress later sequential repeats, so intentional repeated tool use after the first call settles still works.

## Linked context

Related issue: #80040

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Duplicate Codex app-server dynamic-tool requests in the same thread and turn with different `callId`s but identical tool name and arguments now share the first in-flight execution instead of invoking the underlying OpenClaw tool twice.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS 15.6, Node v24.13.1, branch `codex/dedupe-inflight-dynamic-tools`, commit `3af143b53f17`. The probe used the real `createCodexDynamicToolBridge`, `createCodexDynamicToolInFlightCoalescer`, and `handleDynamicToolCallWithTimeout` runtime path with a side-effecting OpenClaw dynamic tool that appends to a temp log file. Channel tokens and live message targets were not used.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` local runtime probe that created one side-effecting OpenClaw dynamic tool, then concurrently submitted two Codex `item/tool/call`-equivalent requests with `callId=call-send-a` and `callId=call-send-b`, same `threadId=proof-thread-2026-05-30`, same `turnId=proof-turn-1`, same tool, and semantically identical arguments with object keys in different order.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the local runtime probe:

```text
OpenClaw Codex dynamic tool in-flight proof
source branch: codex/dedupe-inflight-dynamic-tools
threadId: proof-thread-2026-05-30
turnId: proof-turn-1
request A callId: call-send-a
request B callId: call-send-b
semantic args equal after canonicalization: yes
underlying tool execute count: 1
side-effect log line count: 1
side-effect log:
2026-05-30T02:43:20.065Z callId=call-send-a text=PowerBI daily report ready target=redacted-feishu-channel
first response: {"contentItems":[{"type":"inputText","text":"sent:PowerBI daily report ready"}],"success":true}
duplicate response: {"contentItems":[{"type":"inputText","text":"sent:PowerBI daily report ready"}],"success":true}
same response object: true
```

- Observed result after fix: The first in-flight request stored the execution promise. The duplicate request with the same semantic key joined that promise, the underlying side-effecting tool executed once, the temp side-effect log received one line, and both Codex dynamic-tool responses resolved to the same successful response object.
- What was not tested: No live Feishu, PowerBI, or Gateway channel message was sent from this PR branch. The proof covers the narrow same-turn in-flight duplicate dynamic-tool class. It does not close broader duplicate mechanisms in #80040 such as provider-switch or abort-overlap cleanup, trajectory writer scoping, or LCM double-write behavior.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-execution.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.dynamic-tools.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts`
- `scripts/pre-commit/run-node-tool.sh oxfmt --write --no-error-on-unmatched-pattern extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-execution.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-execution.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(approvals): handle stale plugin waits
target.body:
## Summary
- Extend the shared approval-not-found classifier to cover the gateway waitDecision stale-id message `approval expired or not found`.
- Reuse that classifier in plugin approval waits for before_tool_call, Codex app-server approvals, native hook relay PermissionRequest approvals, and the existing exec approval wait path.
- Preserve fail-closed behavior for stale plugin approval waits while leaving unrelated gateway errors exceptional or on their existing unavailable path.

Closes #88111.

## Validation
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/approval-errors.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/approval-bridge.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.approval-wait.test.ts` -> 4 Vitest shards passed; 4 files / 97 tests passed.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-request.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.approval-wait.test.ts src/infra/approval-errors.test.ts` -> 2 Vitest shards passed; 4 files / 96 tests passed.
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module ...real gateway stale plugin waitDecision proof...` -> passed; real gateway/client error `approval expired or not found`, `isApprovalNotFoundError=true`.
- `env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false corepack pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main` -> passed.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior addressed:** Stale or missing `plugin.approval.waitDecision` approval ids now fail closed as typed stale/unavailable/deferred approval outcomes instead of surfacing as generic runtime/tool failures.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS with Node 24.15.0 and current PR head `f78c8ae5c7f3eb2783f84c252fd1ca31e4db3828`; started a real loopback OpenClaw gateway process from this checkout with an isolated temporary config/state dir and real token auth.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a real gateway with `createOpenClawTestInstance(...).startGateway()`, connected a real gateway client with `operator.approvals` scope, then called the actual gateway/client request path through `client.request("plugin.approval.waitDecision", { id: "plugin:proof-stale-id" }, { timeoutMs: 5000 })` against an intentionally stale/missing plugin approval id. After catching the live gateway error, checked it with the patched shared `isApprovalNotFoundError` classifier. Also ran the focused approval harnesses and the changed-file type/lint/import-cycle gate.
- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted terminal output from the after-fix real-gateway run:
```text
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module - <<'SCRIPT'
...starts createOpenClawTestInstance gateway and calls plugin.approval.waitDecision with plugin:proof-stale-id...
SCRIPT
[proof] real gateway started on loopback test port
[proof] connected gateway client with operator.approvals scope
[proof] call: plugin.approval.waitDecision id=plugin:proof-stale-id
[proof] gateway/client error: approval expired or not found
[proof] isApprovalNotFoundError=true
[proof] result: stale plugin approval id is recognized by patched approval-not-found classifier
[proof] cleanup: stopped gateway and removed temporary test state
```
Additional focused harness output:
```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/approval-errors.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/approval-bridge.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.approval-wait.test.ts
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 16.70s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-request.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.approval-wait.test.ts src/infra/approval-errors.test.ts
[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 6.38s

$ env OPENCLAW_CHECK_CHANGED_REMOTE_CHILD=1 OPENCLAW_CHANGED_LANES_RAW_SYNC=1 CI=1 PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false corepack pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main
[check:changed] lanes=core, coreTests, extensions, extensionTests
[check:changed] typecheck core
[check:changed] typecheck core tests
[check:changed] typecheck extensions
[check:changed] typecheck extension tests
[check:changed] lint core

Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).
```
- **Observed result after fix:** The real gateway/client stale-id path produced `approval expired or not found`, and the patched shared classifier returned `true` for that live error. The patched before_tool_call path returns `Plugin approval unavailable (stale approval)` and blocks even when `timeoutBehavior` is `allow`; Codex app-server returns `{ decision: "decline" }` and emits an `unavailable` approval event; native hook relay PermissionRequest returns a no-op/defer response instead of throwing.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a browser approval UI or manually restart a long-lived production gateway mid-approval; the real proof uses an isolated local gateway process and a real gateway/client stale approval id response, with the sibling app-server/native/before_tool_call mappings covered by focused source-path tests.

target.title: [codex] tighten final Telegram DM recovery
target.body: ## What changed - Preserve the real durable suppression reason in Telegram final delivery results instead of collapsing every no-send into `no_visible_result`. - Retry the missing-final Telegram recovery path only when the durable send truly reports `no_visible_result`. - Track recovery success separately so a recovered reply is not treated like a failure. - Add coverage for the durable suppression reason and the three Telegram retry cases. ## Why This prevents intentional suppressions from being replayed as if they were delivery misses, while still recovering the real missing-final DM case. ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Telegram final DM responses were being recovered too broadly; intentional durable suppressions could be replayed like delivery misses. - Real environment tested: local macOS OpenClaw gateway setup with the Telegram channel enabled and a live direct DM conversation. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: `openclaw gateway restart`, send a Telegram DM that produces a final reply, then send a case that returns a durable suppression reason. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): terminal/live output from the OpenClaw Telegram conversation showed the reply path completing normally, and the gateway recovery branch only being eligible when the durable send reason was `no_visible_result`. - Screenshot proof of issue: ![Telegram proof screenshot] - Observed result after fix: visible final DM replies were replayed only for `no_visible_result`; intentional suppressions such as `cancelled_by_message_sending_hook` and `no_visible_payload` did not retry. - What was not tested: unrelated channels, non-final reply paths, and other transport-specific suppression reasons outside Telegram final delivery. - Proof limitations or environment constraints: the managed gateway install remains the production target; I did not use the crash-prone `gateway:watch` path for this proof. ## Validation - `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` passed: 95/95 - `src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts` passed: 7/7
