target.title: fix: recover stale diagnostic session lanes
target.body:
## Summary
- release stale per-session command lanes when no embedded run owns the active lane task
- keep the lane only when a shared non-session lane has active or queued work that can explain the wait
- add diagnostic skip reason and focused runtime/integration coverage for stale lane release versus shared-lane backpressure

## Verification
- `PATH=/home/openclaw/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/logging/diagnostic-session-recovery.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts`
- `PATH=/home/openclaw/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts`
- `PATH=/home/openclaw/.openclaw/tools/node-v22.22.0/bin:$PATH node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -b tsconfig.projects.json`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: OpenClaw gateway diagnostic recovery can leave a stale per-session lane active when no embedded run is active, or release it too aggressively while shared lane backpressure explains the wait.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout with Node 22.22.0; patched package was also built and installed locally for the project-flow job-hunter runtime before this PR.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Focused vitest files, oxfmt on touched files, and project-wide tsgo command listed above.
Evidence after fix: Focused tests pass 24/24; tsgo exits 0; local gateway logs show `shared_lane_backpressure` skips instead of incorrect lane release while shared queue work is active.
Observed result after fix: Stale orphaned session lanes can be released when no embedded run owns them, while lanes queued behind shared work are preserved until backpressure clears.
What was not tested: Full OpenClaw `pnpm check`; the local checkout has unrelated release/lock drift, so broad pnpm check was not run here.

target.body:
## Summary
- Add a bundled `capability-manifest-verifier` extension that registers an OpenClaw trusted tool policy.
- Verify broker-issued HS256 JWT capability manifests from env or a configured local path, with secrets kept out of plugin config.
- Map manifest grants to allow, require approval, or deny decisions; unknown tools fail closed by default.
- Add focused tests for signature validation, expiration/agent checks, approval routing, fail-closed behavior, startup metadata, and metadata discovery.

## Safety posture
- No generic tool passthrough or runtime mutation surface is added.
- Secret material is read only from an environment variable and is not emitted in block reasons.
- Invalid, missing, expired, or mismatched manifests block tool calls when the plugin is enabled.
- The bundled plugin is not enabled by default; operators must explicitly enable/configure it.
- The plugin follows the lightweight bundled-plugin shape: no package dependencies, package manifest, lockfile change, or package-boundary workspace opt-in.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: OpenClaw can discover the bundled `capability-manifest-verifier` extension safely by default, and when explicitly enabled its trusted policy enforces allow, approval, deny, missing-grant, expired-token, and agent-mismatch decisions before tool execution.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-capability-manifest-policy` on branch `evaos-capability-manifest-openclaw`, using the source-built OpenClaw Node runtime and the OpenClaw plugin-test runtime helpers.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  - Ran `node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect capability-manifest-verifier --runtime --json` after installing dependencies from the lockfile, then projected the live runtime fields with `jq`.
  - Ran an enabled-policy probe with `node --import tsx --input-type=module`, importing `extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.ts` and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-test-runtime`, signing redacted HS256 manifests in-process, enabling the plugin via `captured.api.pluginConfig`, and evaluating the registered trusted policy against representative tool calls.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the real OpenClaw runtime inspection:

```console
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect capability-manifest-verifier --runtime --json | jq '{id:.plugin.id, origin:.plugin.origin, enabled:.plugin.enabled, activated:.plugin.activated, configSchema:.plugin.configSchema, dependencyStatus:.plugin.dependencyStatus, diagnostics:.diagnostics}'
{
  "id": "capability-manifest-verifier",
  "origin": "bundled",
  "enabled": false,
  "activated": false,
  "configSchema": true,
  "dependencyStatus": {
    "hasDependencies": false,
    "installed": true,
    "requiredInstalled": true,
    "optionalInstalled": true,
    "missing": [],
    "missingOptional": [],
    "dependencies": [],
    "optionalDependencies": []
  },
  "diagnostics": []
}
```

Enabled trusted-policy probe output, with token/secret material redacted and not printed:

```console
$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <enabled-policy-probe>
{"label":"allow grant","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"allow"}
{"label":"approval grant","toolName":"desktop_bridge_codex_send_visible_message","outcome":"requires_approval","title":"Capability manifest approval required","timeoutBehavior":"deny"}
{"label":"deny grant","toolName":"shell_exec","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest denies tool: shell_exec."}
{"label":"missing grant","toolName":"unknown_tool","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest has no grant for tool: unknown_tool."}
{"label":"expired manifest","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest check failed: manifest expired."}
{"label":"agent mismatch","toolName":"desktop_bridge_status","outcome":"block","reason":"Capability manifest check failed: manifest agent mismatch."}
```

Focused runtime test confirmation for the same verifier surface:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts -t "capability-manifest-verifier|trusted tool policy|trusted policy"
Test Files  2 passed | 1 skipped (3)
Tests  18 passed | 78 skipped (96)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The plugin is discoverable as bundled runtime plugin `capability-manifest-verifier`, remains disabled and inactive until explicitly configured, exposes its config schema, reports no missing dependencies, emits no diagnostics, and its enabled trusted policy produces the expected allow, approval, deny, missing, expired, and agent-mismatch outcomes without leaking token or secret material.
- **What was not tested**: Production evaOS broker-issued seeded JWT policy acceptance is intentionally left to the downstream evaOS acceptance path tracked in `electricsheephq/evaos-desktop-bridge#143`; this PR proves the OpenClaw verifier surface and enabled policy behavior in the local source-built runtime.

## Validation
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts src/plugins/installed-plugin-index.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/host-hooks.contract.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.embedded-mode.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/extension-package-project-boundaries.test.ts -t "capability-manifest-verifier|bundled|manifest|metadata|trusted|trusted policy|opt-in extension package boundaries"` -> 7 files, 77 passed, 72 skipped
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/index.test.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/src/config.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/src/manifest.ts extensions/capability-manifest-verifier/openclaw.plugin.json src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts .github/labeler.yml`
- `git diff --check`

## evaOS linkage
Part of the evaOS capability-manifest verifier sprint tracked from `electricsheephq/evaos-desktop-bridge#143`.

target.title: feat(agents): add strict local model lean profile
target.body:
## Summary

- add an experimental `localModelLeanProfile` knob for `agents.defaults.experimental` and per-agent runtime config
- keep `localModelLean: true` on the existing basic profile by default, while allowing `strict` to trim the local-model tool surface to the smallest safe core set
- document the new profile in the experimental feature, local model, and Ollama docs and refresh the generated config baseline

Refs https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599.

## Verification

Behavior addressed: local/small-model operators can opt into a stricter lean tool profile without changing the default `localModelLean` behavior, and per-agent overrides flow through the existing merged config path.

Real environment tested: focused local Vitest wrapper in a linked worktree, generated config baseline check, docs MDX/format checks, formatting/lint checks, branch autoreview, and AWS Crabbox Linux changed gate. Blacksmith Testbox proof remains blocked locally by missing Blacksmith auth, so broad remote proof used direct AWS Crabbox.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/experimental-features.md docs/gateway/local-models.md docs/providers/ollama.md
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/concepts/experimental-features.md docs/gateway/local-models.md docs/providers/ollama.md
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/local-model-lean.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/types.agent-defaults.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/local-model-lean.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.model-provider-collision.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts --reporter=dot
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main
node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --target linux --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 180m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"
```

Evidence after fix: refreshed head `4c6cf440668`; focused local wrapper passed 88 tests across the local-model lean and config schema shards; generated config baseline, docs MDX, docs formatting, oxfmt, oxlint, and diff checks passed; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings with `overall: patch is correct (0.82)`; AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` passed with provider `aws`, lease `cbx_15f991019c55`, slug `quick-krill`, run `run_b23ebf3ae9de`, machine `c7a.8xlarge`, exit `0`, `leaseStopped=true`. The changed gate selected lanes `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.

Observed result after fix: tests prove basic remains the default lean profile, strict only applies when local lean mode is enabled, strict can be set globally or per-agent, invalid enum values are rejected, and provider/tool policy filtering uses the stricter allowlist when selected.

What was not tested: true live Windows local-model reproduction from https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599 was not rerun in this PR. This is an opt-in tool-surface reduction for small/local models, not the direct diagnostics starvation fix.

target.title: fix(prompt): preserve IDENTITY defaults in system prompt
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve explicit IDENTITY.md expression defaults (naming, vibe, preferred emoji) in the system prompt when IDENTITY.md is present
- Add a focused regression test for that prompt guidance text

## Changes

- `src/agents/system-prompt.ts`: detect IDENTITY.md in context files and inject guidance into the project context section
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`: regression test verifying IDENTITY guidance is present when identity file is loaded

## Scope

**2 files only** — no approval SDK, no action renderer changes, no unrelated fixes. This is the minimal scoped fix only.

Rebuilds from the closed #65777, scoped down to the real feature files only.

## Verification

Targeted test:
- `src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts` — all tests pass

## Real behavior proof

Constructed the prompt locally on the PR head with a minimal `IDENTITY.md` context file and verified the new guidance line is injected into the Project Context section.

Reproducer (`./node_modules/.bin/tsx` against PR head):

```ts
import { buildAgentSystemPrompt } from "./src/agents/system-prompt.ts";

const prompt = buildAgentSystemPrompt({
  workspaceDir: "/tmp/openclaw",
  contextFiles: [{ path: "./IDENTITY.md", content: "Emoji: <redacted>\nName: <redacted>" }],
});

const target =
  "If IDENTITY.md is present, preserve its explicit expression defaults";
console.log("contains IDENTITY guidance:", prompt.includes(target));
```

Excerpt of the constructed prompt (line numbers preserved, identity content redacted):

```
   70 | # Project Context
   71 | The following project context files have been loaded:
   72 | If IDENTITY.md is present, preserve its explicit expression defaults such as naming, vibe, and preferred emoji in normal chat replies when they fit the context; do not suppress them by default.
   73 | ## ./IDENTITY.md
   74 | Emoji: <redacted>
   75 | Name: <redacted>
   76 | ## Silent Replies
   77 | When you have nothing to say, respond with ONLY: NO_REPLY
```

Assertion result:

```
contains IDENTITY guidance: true
```

The guidance line appears in the Project Context section exactly where the changed code injects it (immediately after the "context files have been loaded" header), confirming that loading an `IDENTITY.md` context file triggers the new behavior end-to-end.

target.title: fix(codex): omit tool controls without tools
target.body:
## Summary

- Stops the legacy `openai-codex-responses` transport from sending `tool_choice` and `parallel_tool_calls` when the request has no tools.
- Preserves the existing `tool_choice: "auto"` and `parallel_tool_calls: true` payload when tools are present.
- Adds regression coverage for both no-tool and tool-enabled Codex Responses request bodies.

## Context

Hermes hit the same class of Codex Responses bug in https://github.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/pull/32777 and https://github.com/ryanleeai/hermes-agent/pull/16: tool-only controls survived after the request had no tools. OpenClaw's provider-owned native path already avoids the main no-tools shape, but the legacy built-in Codex Responses transport still set the controls unconditionally.

## Verification

- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/llm/providers/openai-codex-responses.ts src/llm/providers/openai-codex-responses.test.ts`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxlint src/llm/providers/openai-codex-responses.ts src/llm/providers/openai-codex-responses.test.ts`
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e "...payload assertion..."`
- `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --target linux --idle-timeout 45m --ttl 90m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` (`provider=aws`, `lease=cbx_0aa2248a72cd`, `run=run_3f2cb22f984d`, exit 0)
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main` (clean, no accepted/actionable findings)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex Responses request payloads without tools no longer include tool-only controls that can make strict Responses backends reject the request.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout plus AWS Crabbox Linux changed gate.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --input-type=module -e "...payload assertion..."`; `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider aws --target linux --idle-timeout 45m --ttl 90m --timing-json --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"`.

Evidence after fix: Source-level payload assertion returned `{"noToolsHasTools":false,"noToolsHasToolChoice":false,"noToolsHasParallel":false,"withToolsLength":1,"withToolsToolChoice":"auto","withToolsParallel":true}`; AWS Crabbox `pnpm check:changed` completed with exit 0.

Observed result after fix: No-tool Codex Responses payloads omit `tools`, `tool_choice`, and `parallel_tool_calls`; tool-enabled payloads still include one converted tool plus `tool_choice: "auto"` and `parallel_tool_calls: true`.

What was not tested: Live OpenAI Codex network call. Local Vitest runs for this file were not usable on this host while other long local Vitest lanes were active; the regression test is included for CI.

target.title: fix(mcp): kill orphaned stdio children on session teardown + auto-reconnect dead transports
target.body:
## Problem

Two MCP stability issues causing cron failures and mid-turn `Not connected` errors:

1. **Orphaned MCP child processes:** When isolated cron sessions end, the CLI runner only closes the HTTP loopback server but leaves `SessionMcpRuntime` stdio children alive until the idle sweep. The `server-cron.ts` isolated wrapper also only cleans browser sessions. This causes process accumulation (observed 78+ orphaned processes between sweeps).

2. **Unrecoverable transport death:** When an MCP child process dies mid-session (OOM, crash, pipe break), the stdio transport's `send()` throws `Not connected` because `stdin` is gone. This error is unrecoverable for the entire turn, even though the server config is still valid and a fresh process could be spawned.

## Fix

### Commit 1: Kill MCP stdio children on isolated session teardown

- `server-cron.ts`: `runIsolatedAgentJob` finally block now also retires the MCP runtime for isolated cron sessions via `retireSessionMcpRuntimeForSessionKey()`
- `cli-runner.ts`: when `cleanupBundleMcpOnRunEnd` is true, retires MCP runtime by session key (falling back to session ID) after `closeMcpLoopbackServer()`

### Commit 2: Auto-reconnect dead MCP server sessions on tool call failure

- `agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.ts`: `callTool()` now catches transport-death errors (`Not connected`, `EPIPE`, `ERR_STREAM_DESTROYED`, `write after end`) and attempts a single reconnection: dispose dead session → resolve fresh transport → connect → retry tool call
- If reconnection fails, the original error is thrown unchanged
- Reconnection is logged at warn level for observability

### Commit 3: Separate reconnect failure from retried tool-call error

Split the inner try/catch into two phases per ClawSweeper's [P2] finding:
- **Phase 1** (try/catch): `connectWithTimeout` only. If this fails, dispose the new session and throw the original transport-death error.
- **Phase 2** (unguarded): retry `client.callTool` on the healthy reconnected session. If this fails, it's a normal MCP error from a live server, not a transport issue, so it propagates directly.

This ensures `sessions` map consistency: a healthy reconnected session is never disposed by a subsequent call failure.

### Commit 4: Guard reconnect against McpError server responses

Added `McpError` instanceof guard before the message-substring classifier to prevent misclassifying healthy MCP server errors as transport deaths.

The MCP SDK throws two distinct error types through `client.callTool()`:
- **Transport death:** plain `Error("Not connected")` from `transport.send()` when `this._process?.stdin` is null (child process died)
- **Server errors:** `McpError` instances (extends Error, `.name='McpError'`, `.code`) from JSON-RPC error responses (healthy server, application-level failure)

Before this fix, a healthy Playwright server returning `McpError("Not connected to browser")` would match the `"Not connected"` substring check, causing an unnecessary reconnect and replay of a potentially non-idempotent tool call. Now `McpError` instances short-circuit to `throw` immediately, and only plain `Error` from dead transports enters the reconnect path.

## Real Behavior Proof

### Orphaned MCP child accumulation (before fix)

Live capture from production OpenClaw instance (Mac Studio M1 Max, 32 GB) showing 3 waves of duplicate MCP children under gateway PID 70237, each spawned by successive cron runs without cleanup:

```
=== BEFORE: Current MCP child processes ===
Timestamp: 2026-05-30T00:18:57Z

MCP child process counts by server:
  PID=46292 PPID=70237 RSS=81104KB  ETIME=03:15 proton-pass
  PID=46300 PPID=70237 RSS=117664KB ETIME=03:14 playwright-public
  PID=46301 PPID=70237 RSS=29344KB  ETIME=03:14 cua-driver
  PID=46309 PPID=70237 RSS=116368KB ETIME=03:14 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46316 PPID=70237 RSS=48160KB  ETIME=03:14 threads-mcp
  PID=46317 PPID=70237 RSS=69120KB  ETIME=03:14 veritas

  PID=46336 PPID=70237 RSS=81104KB  ETIME=02:48 proton-pass (wave 2)
  PID=46345 PPID=70237 RSS=116240KB ETIME=02:47 playwright-public
  PID=46346 PPID=70237 RSS=29312KB  ETIME=02:47 cua-driver
  PID=46354 PPID=70237 RSS=115024KB ETIME=02:47 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46361 PPID=70237 RSS=48048KB  ETIME=02:47 threads-mcp
  PID=46362 PPID=70237 RSS=69360KB  ETIME=02:47 veritas

  PID=46410 PPID=70237 RSS=99248KB  ETIME=01:23 proton-pass (wave 3)
  PID=46418 PPID=70237 RSS=147184KB ETIME=01:23 playwright-public
  PID=46419 PPID=70237 RSS=29264KB  ETIME=01:22 cua-driver
  PID=46426 PPID=70237 RSS=135376KB ETIME=01:22 playwright-logged-in
  PID=46433 PPID=70237 RSS=47904KB  ETIME=01:22 threads-mcp
  PID=46434 PPID=70237 RSS=79184KB  ETIME=01:22 veritas

Summary: 6 servers × 3 waves = 18 orphaned MCP children, ~1.2 GB RSS
Total MCP-like children: 25 (including non-gateway-parented)
```

Three distinct etime cohorts (03:14, 02:47, 01:22) confirm each cron run spawned a full set of 6 MCP servers and left the previous set alive.

### MCP SDK error type analysis (reconnect classifier proof)

Verified against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.29.0` (pinned in OpenClaw's package.json):

**Transport death path** (`StdioClientTransport.send()`):
```javascript
// node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/esm/client/stdio.js
send(message) {
    return new Promise(resolve => {
        if (!this._process?.stdin) {
            throw new Error('Not connected');  // plain Error, NOT McpError
        }
        // ...
    });
}
```

**Server error path** (`McpError` class):
```javascript
// node_modules/@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/dist/esm/types.js
export class McpError extends Error {
    constructor(code, message, data) {
        super(`MCP error ${code}: ${message}`);
        this.name = 'McpError';  // distinguishable via instanceof
    }
}
```

The `instanceof McpError` guard in commit 4 correctly separates these two paths without relying on fragile message-substring matching for the type distinction.

### Config-level mitigation (already deployed)

In addition to the code fix, 11 cron jobs were updated with `toolsAllow` scoping so they only spawn the MCP servers they actually need. Example: `mcp-orphan-cleanup` only runs a python script via `exec`, so `toolsAllow: ["exec", "process"]` prevents it from spawning 6 MCP servers at all.

### CI failure note

The `checks-node-core-fast` failure is `src/security/audit-sandbox-browser.test.ts` (2 tests timing out at 120s waiting for Docker probes). Unrelated to this PR; no MCP files touched by those tests.

target.title: fix(cli): harden Windows node pairing and approvals
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve stable Windows node/device identity across reconnects and same-key metadata refresh approvals, while still rotating unsafe/stale/revoked/mismatched device tokens
- harden node pairing approval authz, pending/paired node presentation, and gateway node-event routing so Windows nodes are less likely to fall back to manual/TUI repair paths
- route devices/nodes/gateway CLI RPC helpers through direct local gateway-client auth for explicit loopback shared token/password calls, without letting stale inactive env/config tokens suppress device-token fallback
- make host-native Windows exec approval get/set payloads work through the dedicated `exec.approvals.node.*` gateway methods, including native payloads that omit `payloadJSON`
- block raw `node.invoke systemExecApprovals.*` / `system.execApprovals.*` policy mutation paths and keep exec policy mutation on audited dedicated methods
- render host-native Windows node exec approval snapshots read-only in Control UI, including normalization for malformed node-supplied rules
- expose host-native node approval snapshots in the public gateway protocol schema so generated/protocol consumers accept the Windows-native response shape
- clarify the `nodes invoke system.run` CLI error so users are pointed to agent `/exec host=node`, not a nonexistent `openclaw exec` command
- make build/package TypeScript helper scripts run through the repo `tsx` loader so Node builds without built-in TypeScript support can complete

## Draft Snapshot
Current pushed draft head: `be9eeea5ba88c6adb45c652482ab5019dc71229a`.

This remains draft for maintainer review, not because of a known validation failure.

## Validation

target.title: fix(cli): retry logs.tail after journal fallback in logs follow
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `openclaw logs --follow` getting stuck on Linux systemd journal fallback after a transient local Gateway RPC outage or Gateway restart.

When implicit local `logs.tail` fails in follow mode, the CLI can use the active systemd journal as a bridge. However, after a journal payload was emitted, the follow loop permanently latched journal mode and stopped probing `logs.tail`. That meant log presentation could remain journal/console-style until the CLI was restarted, even after Gateway RPC recovered.

This keeps journal fallback temporary:
- preserve the journal cursor so repeated fallback reads do not duplicate journal output;
- do not permanently force journal mode after a journal fallback payload;
- retry `logs.tail` on the next follow iteration so recovered RPC switches back to normal Gateway log formatting/source.

## Related

- Related issue: #66841
- Follow-up to #85380, which added active systemd journal fallback to avoid stale configured-file following.

## Behavior

- Implicit local `openclaw logs --follow`:
  - transient RPC failure can emit active systemd journal fallback logs;
  - later successful `logs.tail` resumes normal RPC/file log output;
  - stale configured-file fallback is still not used for follow mode outages.
- Explicit `--url` behavior is unchanged.


## Reviewer / AI review map

- **Scope:** `openclaw logs --follow` source-selection state only; this does not change explicit `--url` behavior or the active systemd journal bridge itself.
- **Before:** one journal fallback payload latched follow mode into journal output and stopped retrying local Gateway `logs.tail`.
- **After:** journal fallback can bridge a transient outage, but the next follow iteration retries `logs.tail`; when RPC recovers, output returns to normal Gateway log formatting/source.
- **Non-overlap:** this is a CLI follow-loop state fix, not a Gateway config/runtime lifecycle change and not a general logging backend rewrite.
- **Review focus:** cursor preservation across repeated fallback reads, removal of the persistent journal latch, and resuming the Gateway cursor after recovered `logs.tail`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** After this patch, implicit local `openclaw logs --follow` retries `logs.tail` after a temporary systemd-journal fallback. Repeated fallback reads preserve the journal cursor, and a later successful `logs.tail` returns the follow session to normal Gateway RPC/file log output instead of staying journal-latched.
- **Real setup tested:** Disposable non-production OpenClaw checkout at PR head `aea512eec0ff0f6ecd096d05c0941041c05956c7`, using a synthetic profile/home, a loopback fake Gateway WebSocket that implements the real Gateway protocol handshake and `logs.tail` method, and fake `systemctl`/`journalctl` binaries at the OS boundary. No production Gateway, live runtime state, live config, or real system service was used.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the CLI startup bundle, started the fake Gateway on loopback, then ran the actual CLI follow command against the disposable profile with synthetic systemd tools first in `PATH`:

  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=19081   HOME=<synthetic-home>   OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<synthetic-home>/.openclaw   OPENCLAW_PROFILE=proof   OPENCLAW_SYSTEMD_UNIT=openclaw-proof-gateway   PATH=<synthetic-bin>:<proof-bin>:$PATH   timeout 8s node openclaw.mjs logs --follow --plain --interval 50 --timeout 700
  ```

  The timeout wrapper only bounded the long-running follow command; the evidence below was emitted before timeout.
- **Evidence after fix:** The fake Gateway log recorded two initial failed `logs.tail` calls, then recovered Gateway RPC calls including cursor resume:

  ```text
  logs.tail call #1 -> terminate-for-fallback
  logs.tail call #2 -> terminate-for-fallback
  logs.tail call #3 -> recovered line cursor=10
  logs.tail call #4 -> cursor=10, recovered line cursor=20
  ```

  The CLI output showed journal fallback lines followed by recovered Gateway RPC/file log lines:

  ```text
  Log source: journalctl --user --boot --user-unit=openclaw-proof-gateway.service _PID=4242
  Service PID: 4242
  Service Unit: openclaw-proof-gateway.service
  first journal bridge line
  second journal bridge line
  2026-06-03T07:45:00.000+00:00 info gateway rpc recovered line cursor=10
  2026-06-03T07:45:00.000+00:00 info gateway rpc recovered line cursor=20
  ```

  The fake `journalctl` invocation log also showed cursor-preserving follow reads:

  ```text
  journalctl ... --show-cursor -n 200
  journalctl ... --show-cursor --after-cursor=s=abc
  journalctl ... --show-cursor --after-cursor=s=def
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The actual CLI process first fell back to the synthetic systemd journal when local `logs.tail` failed, kept retrying Gateway RPC instead of latching onto journal mode, then resumed Gateway log output after the fake Gateway recovered. The recovered RPC call used the Gateway cursor from the prior successful response (`cursor=10`), while journal fallback calls used `--after-cursor`, confirming both cursor paths advance independently.
- **What was not tested:** No production Gateway, no live OpenClaw runtime state, no live config, and no real systemd journal/service. The proof uses a disposable checkout plus synthetic loopback Gateway/systemd boundary so the CLI/runtime path is real while the external OS/Gateway boundary is controlled and public-safe.

target.title: fix(config): cap runtime session-store cache retention
target.body:
# Fix: cap runtime session-store cache retention for oversized stores

## Summary

This patch adds a runtime byte cap to the live session-store object and snapshot caches so oversized `sessions.json` loads do not stay resident as parsed object graphs in the gateway heap.

The on-disk session store remains the source of truth. Large stores can still be reloaded on demand, but they are no longer retained in memory just because they were loaded once.

Scope note: this PR only covers the session-store work in this branch. It does not include the separate bootstrap-cache patch or the separate `webgate` patch from the other branch.

## Issue Evidence

- The gateway previously crashed with a V8 heap OOM on the same machine.
- Before the patch, the live session-store tree and quarantine tree were multi-gigabyte, and the runtime was repeatedly loading the full session store during startup/recovery paths.
- The old behavior kept parsed session-store object graphs and immutable snapshots in memory after load, which is the retention pattern this patch caps.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior or issue addressed: oversized session-store hydration could retain parsed object graphs and snapshots in the live gateway cache, which increased heap pressure during startup, recovery, and repeated session access.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw workspace on the same machine, using the source tree at `/root/.openclaw/openclaw-fork-rebase` and the same runtime files that back the gateway.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- I ran a local `tsx` script against the source tree at `/root/.openclaw/openclaw-fork-rebase`.
- The script created a temporary `sessions.json`, forced `OPENCLAW_SESSION_CACHE_MAX_BYTES=64`, loaded the store twice, and probed the serialized and snapshot caches.
- The temp store used a single oversized session entry; the raw payload is omitted here because the proof only needs cache behavior, not session content.

Evidence after fix:
```text
after save serialized entries= 0
after save snapshot entries= 0
storeReads= 2
sameReference= false
equalPayload= true
serialized entries after loads= 0
snapshot entries after loads= 0
small serialized entries= 1
oversize direct read null= true
after oversized direct read serialized entries= 0
after oversized direct read snapshot entries= 0
```

Observed result after fix: for an oversized store, `0` cache entries is the correct result because the patch intentionally keeps that store out of the runtime caches. Repeated loads still return correct data, and direct oversized cache reads invalidate instead of retaining the object graph in memory. The `small serialized entries= 1` line is the positive control that proves normal-sized stores can still enter the cache. In the live gateway, the process stayed healthy and recovered back down after spikes during normal use; the conservative internal "critical" warnings still fired at times, but the post-GC floor remained bounded in the observed window and did not show the earlier runaway crash pattern.

User observation: under heavy normal use, the gateway also seemed noticeably faster after this patch, with smoother response under load. That is currently anecdotal only and not backed by measurement yet, so it should be treated as a qualitative observation rather than proof.

What was not tested: an overnight soak or a full quarantine/restart-recovery cleanup pass after this specific patch. Supplemental runtime data can be added later if the long-run floor starts drifting.

## Before-Fix Evidence (Supplementary)

<details>
<summary>Pre-patch crash and pressure symptoms</summary>

- The gateway previously crashed with a V8 heap OOM rather than a native fault.
- The live process had repeated memory-pressure warnings before this patch.
- The on-disk session corpus was large enough to justify avoiding persistent in-memory retention of oversized stores.

</details>

target.title: fix(cron): capture originating session/agent on the cron wake tool call
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** The cron `wake` MCP tool forwards only `{mode, text}` to the gateway, so every wake enqueues against the heartbeat/main default. Wakes scheduled from a non-main session (Telegram topic, Discord thread, multi-agent setup) silently route to the wrong conversation lane — the event text never returns to the conversation that scheduled it. Closes the tool-path half of #46886 / #64556.
- **What changed.**
  - `cron-tool.ts` wake action resolves the calling agent's `sessionKey`/`agentId` via the existing `resolveInternalSessionKey` / `resolveSessionAgentId` pattern (mirrors the `add` action). Explicit tool params take precedence; an explicit cross-agent `sessionKey` derives its `agentId` from the key itself (`parseAgentSessionKey`) so the caller's agentId is never paired with a foreign session.
  - `WakeParamsSchema` (+ regenerated Swift protocol client), gateway wake handler, cron service contract, and `wake()` thread the optional `sessionKey`/`agentId` through to `enqueueSystemEvent` and the heartbeat request.
  - `wake()` carries the originating session's **stored channel delivery context** (e.g. the bound Telegram `message_thread_id`) onto the system event via an optional `resolveOriginDeliveryContext` dep, implemented in `server-cron.ts` from the **session store** (not by string-splitting the session key) — a wake-now heartbeat replies in the originating topic, not the chat root. No-origin wakes keep the exact pre-fix `enqueueSystemEvent(text, undefined)` shape.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Rebase onto current main (this push)

- Rebased onto current `main`. Main has since absorbed the *scheduled-job* half of this design natively: `sessionTarget:"main"` cron jobs now run through the dedicated `agent:<id>:cron:<job>:run:<ts>` lane and borrow the target session's delivery context (`resolveMainSessionCronDeliveryContext`). **This PR therefore no longer touches `executeMainSessionCronJob`/the run lane at all** — it layers the same origin-capture idea onto the manual `wake` path only, which main still lacks. (This resolves the earlier ClawSweeper [P1] "Preserve main cron run lanes" by construction.)
- `wake()` moved to `src/cron/service/wake.ts` upstream; the change follows it there.
- Swift gateway protocol client regenerated (`pnpm protocol:gen && pnpm protocol:gen:swift`) for the new `WakeParams.agentId` field — resolves the earlier [P2].

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A cron `wake` scheduled from a non-main Telegram forum-topic session must enqueue against the originating session, and the wake-triggered heartbeat turn must deliver its reply back into that same topic instead of the heartbeat/main default (tool-path half of #46886 / #64556).
- **Real environment tested:** Real OpenClaw gateway built from this head (`pnpm build:docker`), real Telegram Bot API (dedicated test bot) and a real Telegram Desktop client driven by the tdlib user driver, running in a Linux container; `claude-cli` (claude-sonnet-4-6) agent backend; BEFORE (`57633c42b`, current main) and AFTER (this branch) builds captured with identical config including `agents.defaults.heartbeat: {"every":"1h","target":"last"}`. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop-proof flow (telegram-desktop + user-driver + ffmpeg screen recording).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** In a fresh forum topic, the driver account sends: *"I am testing my cron wake routing in this topic. Please schedule a wake for right now using your cron tool (action wake, mode now) with text: You were woken by a cron wake. Run date -u with your exec tool and reply with exactly PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at followed by the command output. After your wake tool call returns, reply only: scheduled."* — recorded by ffmpeg with the gateway at debug log level.
- **Evidence after fix:** screen recording [after-83738v2-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-small.gif) and tool-bearing wake-turn recording [after-83738v3-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-small.gif); screenshots [after v2 still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-full.png), [after v3 still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-full.png); raw mp4s [v2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-raw.mp4), [v3](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-raw.mp4); redacted runtime logs [gw-after v2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v2.log), [gw-after v3](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v3.log).
- **Observed result after fix:** the turn reply "scheduled." lands in the topic (`outbound send ok … threadId=74 messageId=76`); the wake fires an immediate heartbeat turn ON the topic session (`trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present`); that second agent turn runs `date -u` via the exec tool and its reply — "PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at Wed Jun 10 04:43:21 UTC 2026" — is delivered into the SAME topic (`threadId=74 messageId=77`). Live command output in the reply demonstrates a real freshly-executed agent turn, not a delayed canned message.
- **Before evidence (optional):** [before-83738v2-small.gif](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-small.gif), [before still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-full.png), [before mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-raw.mp4), [gw-before log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-before-83738v2.log) — same scenario on current main: "scheduled." arrives, then the wake-triggered heartbeat runs detached (`historyPrompt=none`) and its reply is never delivered to the topic or the chat root.
- **What was not tested:** Discord/Matrix live wake routing (the deliveryContext path is channel-generic; pinned by `wake-origin-delivery.test.ts`); live cross-agent wakes (the agentId-derivation contract is pinned by the cron-tool wake-routing unit tests).

### Current-head proof — no heartbeat target configured (the `a53fddb9f59` fallback)

Driven on the exact PR head with **no `agents.defaults.heartbeat` block at all**, via the same `wake` RPC Mantis uses (`system event --mode now --session-key <topic>`). The wake-triggered turn runs `date -u` and its reply lands back in the originating forum topic — proving the new no-heartbeat-target fallback:

![no-target wake reply in topic](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-small.gif)

Reply captured live: **"PROOF-83738 no-target wake ran at Wed Jun 10 13:14:33 UTC 2026"** ([full still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-full.png) · [mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/notarget-rpc-raw.mp4)). Gateway debug log ([gw-notarget.log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-notarget.log)) shows the chain with no heartbeat target set:

```
[telegram] Inbound …topic:130 …                                     <- seed turn
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec … trigger=user …
[telegram] outbound send ok … threadId=130                          <- seed reply "ready"
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec … trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present   <- wake turn ran ON the topic session
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=133 … threadId=130          <- wake reply, SAME topic
```

**`target: "none"` still suppresses** ([gw-tnone.log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-tnone.log)): with `heartbeat.target: "none"`, the same wake fires the heartbeat turn (`trigger=heartbeat`, `outBytes=12`) but produces **zero** `outbound send` — operator opt-out preserved.

### Proof detail — Mantis-flow recreation

This is a recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop-proof flow using the patched local crabbox (same mechanism: real Telegram Desktop client + tdlib user driver + ffmpeg screen capture in a Linux container; both builds, same config incl. `agents.defaults.heartbeat: {"every":"1h","target":"last"}`, bot + group ids are test-only). Scenario: a user in a **forum topic** asks the agent to schedule `cron wake (mode "now")`; the wake text instructs the heartbeat to reply in-thread.

| | BEFORE (current main, `57633c42b`) | AFTER (this branch, `880d10a34`) |
|---|---|---|
| turn reply ("scheduled.") | ✅ in topic | ✅ in topic |
| wake-triggered heartbeat reply | ❌ **never delivered anywhere** (not topic, not chat root) | ✅ **"PROOF-83738 wake delivered." lands back in the same topic** |

| BEFORE | AFTER |
|---|---|
| ![before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-small.gif) | ![after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-small.gif) |

**The wake starts a real second agent TURN, not a delayed canned reply** — in this capture the wake text instructs the woken turn to run `date -u` via the exec tool, and the in-topic reply embeds the live command output ("PROOF-83738 wake turn ran at Wed Jun 10 04:43:21 UTC 2026"), which only a freshly-executed agent turn can produce:

![wake turn runs a tool](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-small.gif)

([full still](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-full.png) · [mp4](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v3-raw.mp4) · [gateway debug log](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v3.log))

Full-window stills: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-full.png) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-full.png) · raw mp4s: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/before-83738v2-raw.mp4) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/after-83738v2-raw.mp4) · redacted gateway logs: [before](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-before-83738v2.log) / [after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof-assets-83738/gw-after-83738v2.log)

Key gateway-log lines (AFTER build, debug level):

```
[telegram] Inbound message telegram:group:-100…:topic:74 -> @…2bot (group, 227 chars)
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec: … trigger=user …
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=76 … threadId=74            <- turn reply "scheduled."
[agent/cli-backend] cli exec: … trigger=heartbeat … historyPrompt=present   <- the wake fired, ON the topic session
[telegram] outbound send ok … messageId=77 … threadId=74            <- wake reply, SAME topic
```

Same scenario on BEFORE: the heartbeat fires with `historyPrompt=none` on a detached lane and no outbound send ever follows — the wake is lost.

A deterministic harness driving the real gateway handler + cron service with logging deps is included (`pnpm exec tsx scripts/proof-cron-wake-origin.mts`): origin routing, backwards-compatible default routing, the next-heartbeat targeted-immediate collapse, the subagent guard, and whitespace fall-through.

## Mutation testing (focused Stryker on the PR-touched code)

- `src/cron/service/wake.ts`: **51/51 mutants killed (100%)**. Two initial survivors exposed real test gaps (agentId-only wakes; sessionKey-only delivery-context resolution) — both now pinned by dedicated tests.
- `src/agents/tools/cron-tool.ts` wake arm (L840–893): **26/31 killed (83.9%)**. All 5 survivors are test-harness equivalents, not behavior gaps: the suite `vi.mock`s `resolveSessionAgentId` to a constant (arg mutations invisible), the `mode` fallback equals the compared literal, and `{expectFinal:false}` is invisible to the gateway mock. The killable gaps found on the way (wake-without-text guard, context-less callers, explicit next-heartbeat mode) got tests.

## Tests / CI

- New/updated: `wake-origin.test.ts`, `wake-origin-delivery.test.ts`, cron-tool wake-routing suite (10 cases), `validateWakeParams` schema cases. All wake/cron/protocol suites green on Windows and Linux.
- Full Linux test suite run in the proof container on this head: unit config fully green (1502/1502); extensions + full vitest green except `extensions/bonjour/advertiser.test.ts` flaking under parallel load (passes in isolation) and `src/agents/compaction-planning-worker.test.ts` (packaged-worker test, fails identically on unmodified upstream main in the same container — pre-existing environmental). `src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts` 46/46 green.
- Windows prepush mirror: `pnpm check` (lint, typecheck prod), `build:strict-smoke`, protocol-gen mirror, plugins assets all green; the 11 extension test files that fail locally on Windows are pre-existing path-separator environment failures (all 191 tests in those files pass on Linux on this head, unchanged by this PR).

target.title: fix(cli): replace hardcoded "--" with FLAG_TERMINATOR constant in getCommandPathInternal
target.body:
## Summary

Replace the sole remaining hardcoded `"--"` literal in `getCommandPathInternal` with the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant already imported and used by every other function in `argv.ts`.

Prevents silent subcommand-routing divergence if `FLAG_TERMINATOR` is ever changed.

**Addressed review feedback (ClawSweeper P3):** Reworded the regression test comment to accurately describe what it proves (integration with the constant), since the test cannot independently detect a literal regression while `FLAG_TERMINATOR` remains `"--"`. The companion contract test asserts the canonical value.

## Linked context

Closes #83902

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed:** `getCommandPathInternal` used a hardcoded `"--"` string while all other functions in the same file use the `FLAG_TERMINATOR` constant.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (050c0813b)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
npx tsx proof-89897.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
=== Live Proof: #89897 / #83902 ===
FLAG_TERMINATOR constant: "--"

Case 1: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, channels, --, add], 2)
  Result: [channels]
  PASS: true

Case 2: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, channels, add], 2)
  Result: [channels, add]
  PASS: true

Case 3: getCommandPath([node, openclaw, config, --, set, --key, value], 2)
  Result: [config]
  PASS: true

Case 4: getCommandPathWithRootOptions([node, openclaw, --debug, status, --, extra], 2)
  Result: [status]
  PASS: true

Case 5: FLAG_TERMINATOR === "--"
  PASS: true

=== Live Proof #89897: ALL PASSED ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** FLAG_TERMINATOR correctly stops command-path parsing. Command-path resolution is consistent with all other argv helpers.
- **What was not tested:** N/A — single-line refactor with focused regression tests and live CLI behavior proof.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/cli/argv.test.ts --reporter=verbose
# 112 passed (1 test file)
```

**What regression coverage was added or updated?**
- `"uses FLAG_TERMINATOR constant to stop command-path parsing"` — exercises integration with the imported constant
- `"FLAG_TERMINATOR is the canonical -- string"` — contract test asserting the canonical value

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** No
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** No
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** No
**What is the highest-risk area?** None — identical runtime behavior (same constant value `"--"`).
**How is that risk mitigated?** 112 argv tests pass; the contract test explicitly asserts the canonical value.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Requesting ClawSweeper re-review.
**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** None — live CLI behavior proof added.

---

Co-Authored-By: Claude Code <noreply@anthropic.com>

target.title: fix(web_fetch): strip whitespace from URLs to prevent new URL() parse errors
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91651: LLMs occasionally generate `web_fetch` URLs with accidental spaces (e.g. `"https:// docs.example.com/page"`) which cause `new URL()` to throw `Invalid URL: must be http or https`.

**Revised approach** (per ClawSweeper review): Instead of unconditionally stripping all whitespace, the fix uses try-catch fallback:
1. Try `new URL(params.url)` first — preserves standard percent-encoding for valid URLs
2. Only if parsing fails, fall back to whitespace-stripped URL — recovers from LLM-generated space typos
3. This ensures valid URLs with path/query spaces (e.g. `https://example.com/a%20b`) keep their percent-encoding instead of being silently rewritten

## Linked context

Closes #91651

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `web_fetch` tool rejects URLs with accidental internal whitespace (LLM generation artifact). Old approach was too aggressive (stripped all whitespace, breaking valid URLs). New try-catch approach preserves valid URL semantics.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS, Node v22.22.3, latest upstream/main (050c0813b)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
node proof-91693.mjs
```

- **Evidence after fix (copied live output):**

```
=== URL Parsing Fix: try-catch fallback ===

Case 1: LLM-generated whitespace
  Input:  "https:// docs.example.com/page"
  Raw URL() throws: true
  Recovered href: https://docs.example.com/page
  PASS: true

Case 2: Tab after scheme
  Input:  "https://\texample.com/path"
  Recovered href: https://example.com/path
  PASS: true

Case 3: Valid URL with path/query spaces
  Input:  "https://example.com/a b/search?q=hello world"
  Raw URL() succeeds: true
  Result href: https://example.com/a%20b/search?q=hello%20world
  Path spaces encoded: true  Query spaces encoded: true
  PASS: true (spaces preserved as %20, NOT stripped)

Case 4: Normal URL (no-op)
  Input:  "https://example.com/normal"
  Result href: https://example.com/normal
  PASS: true

=== Live HTTP fetch with space-stripped URL ===

Malformed URL: "https:// example.com"
Cleaned URL:   "https://example.com"
HTTP status: 200
Content-length: chunked
Body preview: <!doctype html><html lang="en"><head><title>Example Domain</title>...
Live fetch: PASS

=== URL parsing: ALL PASSED ===
=== Proof #91693: COMPLETE ===
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Malformed URLs are recovered via whitespace stripping, while valid URLs with path/query spaces retain correct percent-encoding. Live HTTP fetch confirms the cleaned URL resolves.
- **What was not tested:** All HTTP URLs that could be affected by whitespace removal (infinite combinatorial space). The fix uses standard URL parser behavior for valid URLs.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live fetch depends on network access; URL parsing logic is deterministic and verified across 4 edge cases.

## Tests and validation

**Which commands did you run?**

target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`
